{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:29","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: Augmented-Reality Enabled Crop Monitoring with Robot Assistance\nauthor: Mucchiani; Caio; Chatziparaschis; Dimitrios; Karydis; Konstantinos\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.03483v1\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: The integration of augmented reality (AR), extended reality (XR), and virtual reality (VR) technologies in agriculture has shown significant promise in enhancing various agricultural practices. Mobile robots have also been adopted as assessment tools in precision agriculture, improving economic efficiency and productivity, and minimizing undesired effects such as weeds and pests. Despite considerable work on both fronts, the combination of a versatile User Interface (UI) provided by an AR headset with the integration and direct interaction and control of a mobile field robot has not yet been fully explored or standardized. This work aims to address this gap by providing real-time data input and control output of a mobile robot for precision agriculture through a virtual environment enabled by an AR headset interface. The system leverages open-source computational tools and off-the-shelf hardware for effective integration. Distinctive case studies are presented where growers or technicians can interact with a legged robot via an AR headset and a UI. Users can teleoperate the robot to gather information in an area of interest, request real-time graphed status of an area, or have the robot autonomously navigate to selected areas for measurement updates. The proposed system utilizes a custom local navigation method with a fixed holographic coordinate system in combination with QR codes. This step toward fusing AR and robotics in agriculture aims to provide practical solutions for real-time data management and control enabled by human-robot interaction. The implementation can be extended to various robot applications in agriculture and beyond, promoting a unified framework for on-demand and autonomous robot operation in the field.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2024-11-05\nlicense: CC BY 4.0\n---\nComputer Science > Robotics\n[Submitted on 5 Nov 2024]\nTitle:Augmented-Reality Enabled Crop Monitoring with Robot Assistance\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:The integration of augmented reality (AR), extended reality (XR), and virtual reality (VR) technologies in agriculture has shown significant promise in enhancing various agricultural practices. Mobile robots have also been adopted as assessment tools in precision agriculture, improving economic efficiency and productivity, and minimizing undesired effects such as weeds and pests. Despite considerable work on both fronts, the combination of a versatile User Interface (UI) provided by an AR headset with the integration and direct interaction and control of a mobile field robot has not yet been fully explored or standardized. This work aims to address this gap by providing real-time data input and control output of a mobile robot for precision agriculture through a virtual environment enabled by an AR headset interface. The system leverages open-source computational tools and off-the-shelf hardware for effective integration. Distinctive case studies are presented where growers or technicians can interact with a legged robot via an AR headset and a UI. Users can teleoperate the robot to gather information in an area of interest, request real-time graphed status of an area, or have the robot autonomously navigate to selected areas for measurement updates. The proposed system utilizes a custom local navigation method with a fixed holographic coordinate system in combination with QR codes. This step toward fusing AR and robotics in agriculture aims to provide practical solutions for real-time data management and control enabled by human-robot interaction. The implementation can be extended to various robot applications in agriculture and beyond, promoting a unified framework for on-demand and autonomous robot operation in the field.\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:30","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: When Dynamic Data Selection Meets Data Augmentation\nauthor: Yang; Suorong; Ye; Peng; Shen; Furao; Zhou; Dongzhan\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.03809v1\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: Dynamic data selection aims to accelerate training with lossless performance. However, reducing training data inherently limits data diversity, potentially hindering generalization. While data augmentation is widely used to enhance diversity, it is typically not optimized in conjunction with selection. As a result, directly combining these techniques fails to fully exploit their synergies. To tackle the challenge, we propose a novel online data training framework that, for the first time, unifies dynamic data selection and augmentation, achieving both training efficiency and enhanced performance. Our method estimates each sample's joint distribution of local density and multimodal semantic consistency, allowing for the targeted selection of augmentation-suitable samples while suppressing the inclusion of noisy or ambiguous data. This enables a more significant reduction in dataset size without sacrificing model generalization. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches on various benchmark datasets and architectures, e.g., reducing 50\\% training costs on ImageNet-1k with lossless performance. Furthermore, our approach enhances noise resistance and improves model robustness, reinforcing its practical utility in real-world scenarios.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2025-05-02\n---\nComputer Science > Machine Learning\n[Submitted on 2 May 2025]\nTitle:When Dynamic Data Selection Meets Data Augmentation\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:Dynamic data selection aims to accelerate training with lossless performance. However, reducing training data inherently limits data diversity, potentially hindering generalization. While data augmentation is widely used to enhance diversity, it is typically not optimized in conjunction with selection. As a result, directly combining these techniques fails to fully exploit their synergies. To tackle the challenge, we propose a novel online data training framework that, for the first time, unifies dynamic data selection and augmentation, achieving both training efficiency and enhanced performance. Our method estimates each sample's joint distribution of local density and multimodal semantic consistency, allowing for the targeted selection of augmentation-suitable samples while suppressing the inclusion of noisy or ambiguous data. This enables a more significant reduction in dataset size without sacrificing model generalization. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches on various benchmark datasets and architectures, e.g., reducing 50\\% training costs on ImageNet-1k with lossless performance. Furthermore, our approach enhances noise resistance and improves model robustness, reinforcing its practical utility in real-world scenarios.\nCurrent browse context:\ncs.LG\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\nIArxiv Recommender\n(What is IArxiv?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? We introduce an adaptive wavelet-based algorithm that performs sequential prediction without prior knowledge of $(s,p,q)$, and establish minimax-optimal regret bounds against any comparator in $B\\_{pq}^s$. We further design a locally adaptive extension capable of dynamically tracking spatially inhomogeneous smoothness. We introduce an adaptive wavelet-based algorithm that performs sequential prediction without prior knowledge of $(s,p,q)$, and establish minimax-optimal regret bounds against any comparator in $B\\_{pq}^s$. We further design a locally adaptive extension capable of dynamically tracking spatially inhomogeneous smoothness. This adaptive mechanism adjusts the resolution of the predictions over both time and space, yielding refined regret bounds in terms of local regularity. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:32","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: Inverse Reinforcement Learning with Switching Rewards and History Dependency for Characterizing Animal Behaviors\nauthor: Ke; Jingyang; Wu; Feiyang; Wang; Jiyi; Markowitz; Jeffrey; Anqi\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.12633v3\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: Traditional approaches to studying decision-making in neuroscience focus on simplified behavioral tasks where animals perform repetitive, stereotyped actions to receive explicit rewards. While informative, these methods constrain our understanding of decision-making to short timescale behaviors driven by explicit goals. In natural environments, animals exhibit more complex, long-term behaviors driven by intrinsic motivations that are often unobservable. Recent works in time-varying inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) aim to capture shifting motivations in long-term, freely moving behaviors. However, a crucial challenge remains: animals make decisions based on their history, not just their current state. To address this, we introduce SWIRL (SWitching IRL), a novel framework that extends traditional IRL by incorporating time-varying, history-dependent reward functions. SWIRL models long behavioral sequences as transitions between short-term decision-making processes, each governed by a unique reward function. SWIRL incorporates biologically plausible history dependency to capture how past decisions and environmental contexts shape behavior, offering a more accurate description of animal decision-making. We apply SWIRL to simulated and real-world animal behavior datasets and show that it outperforms models lacking history dependency, both quantitatively and qualitatively. This work presents the first IRL model to incorporate history-dependent policies and rewards to advance our understanding of complex, naturalistic decision-making in animals.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2025-01-22\nlicense: CC BY-NC-ND 4.0\n---\nComputer Science > Machine Learning\n[Submitted on 22 Jan 2025 (v1), last revised 15 Jul 2025 (this version, v3)]\nTitle:Inverse Reinforcement Learning with Switching Rewards and History Dependency for Characterizing Animal Behaviors\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:Traditional approaches to studying decision-making in neuroscience focus on simplified behavioral tasks where animals perform repetitive, stereotyped actions to receive explicit rewards. While informative, these methods constrain our understanding of decision-making to short timescale behaviors driven by explicit goals. In natural environments, animals exhibit more complex, long-term behaviors driven by intrinsic motivations that are often unobservable. Recent works in time-varying inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) aim to capture shifting motivations in long-term, freely moving behaviors. However, a crucial challenge remains: animals make decisions based on their history, not just their current state. To address this, we introduce SWIRL (SWitching IRL), a novel framework that extends traditional IRL by incorporating time-varying, history-dependent reward functions. SWIRL models long behavioral sequences as transitions between short-term decision-making processes, each governed by a unique reward function. SWIRL incorporates biologically plausible history dependency to capture how past decisions and environmental contexts shape behavior, offering a more accurate description of animal decision-making. We apply SWIRL to simulated and real-world animal behavior datasets and show that it outperforms models lacking history dependency, both quantitatively and qualitatively. This work presents the first IRL model to incorporate history-dependent policies and rewards to advance our understanding of complex, naturalistic decision-making in animals.\nSubmission history\nFrom: Jingyang Ke [view email][v1] Wed, 22 Jan 2025 04:38:33 UTC (10,301 KB)\n[v2] Tue, 3 Jun 2025 17:35:00 UTC (8,871 KB)\n[v3] Tue, 15 Jul 2025 15:08:23 UTC (8,455 KB)\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nPapers with Code (What is Papers with Code?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\nIArxiv Recommender\n(What is IArxiv?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? The deformation tensor is assumed to satisfy the neo-Hookean linear elasticity and degenerates in the normal direction on the solid wall. The uniform estimates in Mach number are established based on two important observations. First, the entropy has enhanced regularity in the direction of each column of the deformation tensor, which exactly helps us avoid the loss of derivatives caused by the simultaneous appearance of elasticity and entropy in vorticity analysis. Second, a special structure of the wave equation of the pressure together with elliptic estimates helps us reduce the normal derivatives in the control of divergence and pressure. The strong convergence of solutions in time is obtained by proving local energy decay of the wave equation and using the technique of microlocal defect measure.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2024-12-13\nlicense: CC BY 4.0\n---\nMathematics > Analysis of PDEs\n[Submitted on 13 Dec 2024]\nTitle:Low Mach Number Limit of Non-isentropic Inviscid Elastodynamics with General Initial Data\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:We prove the incompressible limit of non-isentropic inviscid elastodynamic equations with general initial data in 3D half-space. The deformation tensor is assumed to satisfy the neo-Hookean linear elasticity and degenerates in the normal direction on the solid wall. The uniform estimates in Mach number are established based on two important observations. First, the entropy has enhanced regularity in the direction of each column of the deformation tensor, which exactly helps us avoid the loss of derivatives caused by the simultaneous appearance of elasticity and entropy in vorticity analysis. Second, a special structure of the wave equation of the pressure together with elliptic estimates helps us reduce the normal derivatives in the control of divergence and pressure. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:34","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: Augmented-Reality Enabled Crop Monitoring with Robot Assistance\nauthor: Mucchiani; Caio; Chatziparaschis; Dimitrios; Karydis; Konstantinos\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.03483v1\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: The integration of augmented reality (AR), extended reality (XR), and virtual reality (VR) technologies in agriculture has shown significant promise in enhancing various agricultural practices. Mobile robots have also been adopted as assessment tools in precision agriculture, improving economic efficiency and productivity, and minimizing undesired effects such as weeds and pests. Despite considerable work on both fronts, the combination of a versatile User Interface (UI) provided by an AR headset with the integration and direct interaction and control of a mobile field robot has not yet been fully explored or standardized. This work aims to address this gap by providing real-time data input and control output of a mobile robot for precision agriculture through a virtual environment enabled by an AR headset interface. The system leverages open-source computational tools and off-the-shelf hardware for effective integration. Distinctive case studies are presented where growers or technicians can interact with a legged robot via an AR headset and a UI. Users can teleoperate the robot to gather information in an area of interest, request real-time graphed status of an area, or have the robot autonomously navigate to selected areas for measurement updates. The proposed system utilizes a custom local navigation method with a fixed holographic coordinate system in combination with QR codes. This step toward fusing AR and robotics in agriculture aims to provide practical solutions for real-time data management and control enabled by human-robot interaction. The implementation can be extended to various robot applications in agriculture and beyond, promoting a unified framework for on-demand and autonomous robot operation in the field.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2024-11-05\nlicense: CC BY 4.0\n---\nComputer Science > Robotics\n[Submitted on 5 Nov 2024]\nTitle:Augmented-Reality Enabled Crop Monitoring with Robot Assistance\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:The integration of augmented reality (AR), extended reality (XR), and virtual reality (VR) technologies in agriculture has shown significant promise in enhancing various agricultural practices. Mobile robots have also been adopted as assessment tools in precision agriculture, improving economic efficiency and productivity, and minimizing undesired effects such as weeds and pests. Despite considerable work on both fronts, the combination of a versatile User Interface (UI) provided by an AR headset with the integration and direct interaction and control of a mobile field robot has not yet been fully explored or standardized. This work aims to address this gap by providing real-time data input and control output of a mobile robot for precision agriculture through a virtual environment enabled by an AR headset interface. The system leverages open-source computational tools and off-the-shelf hardware for effective integration. Distinctive case studies are presented where growers or technicians can interact with a legged robot via an AR headset and a UI. Users can teleoperate the robot to gather information in an area of interest, request real-time graphed status of an area, or have the robot autonomously navigate to selected areas for measurement updates. The proposed system utilizes a custom local navigation method with a fixed holographic coordinate system in combination with QR codes. This step toward fusing AR and robotics in agriculture aims to provide practical solutions for real-time data management and control enabled by human-robot interaction. The implementation can be extended to various robot applications in agriculture and beyond, promoting a unified framework for on-demand and autonomous robot operation in the field.\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:35","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: When Dynamic Data Selection Meets Data Augmentation\nauthor: Yang; Suorong; Ye; Peng; Shen; Furao; Zhou; Dongzhan\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.03809v1\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: Dynamic data selection aims to accelerate training with lossless performance. However, reducing training data inherently limits data diversity, potentially hindering generalization. While data augmentation is widely used to enhance diversity, it is typically not optimized in conjunction with selection. As a result, directly combining these techniques fails to fully exploit their synergies. To tackle the challenge, we propose a novel online data training framework that, for the first time, unifies dynamic data selection and augmentation, achieving both training efficiency and enhanced performance. Our method estimates each sample's joint distribution of local density and multimodal semantic consistency, allowing for the targeted selection of augmentation-suitable samples while suppressing the inclusion of noisy or ambiguous data. This enables a more significant reduction in dataset size without sacrificing model generalization. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches on various benchmark datasets and architectures, e.g., reducing 50\\% training costs on ImageNet-1k with lossless performance. Furthermore, our approach enhances noise resistance and improves model robustness, reinforcing its practical utility in real-world scenarios.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2025-05-02\n---\nComputer Science > Machine Learning\n[Submitted on 2 May 2025]\nTitle:When Dynamic Data Selection Meets Data Augmentation\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:Dynamic data selection aims to accelerate training with lossless performance. However, reducing training data inherently limits data diversity, potentially hindering generalization. While data augmentation is widely used to enhance diversity, it is typically not optimized in conjunction with selection. As a result, directly combining these techniques fails to fully exploit their synergies. To tackle the challenge, we propose a novel online data training framework that, for the first time, unifies dynamic data selection and augmentation, achieving both training efficiency and enhanced performance. Our method estimates each sample's joint distribution of local density and multimodal semantic consistency, allowing for the targeted selection of augmentation-suitable samples while suppressing the inclusion of noisy or ambiguous data. This enables a more significant reduction in dataset size without sacrificing model generalization. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches on various benchmark datasets and architectures, e.g., reducing 50\\% training costs on ImageNet-1k with lossless performance. Furthermore, our approach enhances noise resistance and improves model robustness, reinforcing its practical utility in real-world scenarios.\nCurrent browse context:\ncs.LG\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\nIArxiv Recommender\n(What is IArxiv?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? We introduce an adaptive wavelet-based algorithm that performs sequential prediction without prior knowledge of $(s,p,q)$, and establish minimax-optimal regret bounds against any comparator in $B\\_{pq}^s$. We further design a locally adaptive extension capable of dynamically tracking spatially inhomogeneous smoothness. This adaptive mechanism adjusts the resolution of the predictions over both time and space, yielding refined regret bounds in terms of local regularity. Consequently, in heterogeneous environments, our adaptive guarantees can significantly surpass those obtained by standard global methods.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2025-05-26\n---\nMathematics > Statistics Theory\n[Submitted on 26 May 2025 (v1), last revised 22 Sep 2025 (this version, v2)]\nTitle:Minimax Adaptive Online Nonparametric Regression over Besov Spaces\nView PDFAbstract:We study online adversarial regression with convex losses against a rich class of continuous yet highly irregular prediction rules, modeled by Besov spaces $B\\_{pq}^s$ with general parameters $1 \\leq p,q \\leq \\infty$ and smoothness $s > \\tfrac{d}{p}$. We introduce an adaptive wavelet-based algorithm that performs sequential prediction without prior knowledge of $(s,p,q)$, and establish minimax-optimal regret bounds against any comparator in $B\\_{pq}^s$. We further design a locally adaptive extension capable of dynamically tracking spatially inhomogeneous smoothness. This adaptive mechanism adjusts the resolution of the predictions over both time and space, yielding refined regret bounds in terms of local regularity. This is especially true in enterprise settings, where automated agents hold the promise of a high impact. To fill this gap, we propose WorkArena++, a novel benchmark consisting of 682 tasks corresponding to realistic workflows routinely performed by knowledge workers.…\n▽ More\nThe ability of large language models (LLMs) to mimic human-like intelligence has led to a surge in LLM-based autonomous agents. Though recent LLMs seem capable of planning and reasoning given user instructions, their effectiveness in applying these capabilities for autonomous task solving remains underexplored. This is especially true in enterprise settings, where automated agents hold the promise of a high impact. To fill this gap, we propose WorkArena++, a novel benchmark consisting of 682 tasks corresponding to realistic workflows routinely performed by knowledge workers. WorkArena++ is designed to evaluate the planning, problem-solving, logical/arithmetic reasoning, retrieval, and contextual understanding abilities of web agents. Our empirical studies across state-of-the-art LLMs and vision-language models (VLMs), as well as human workers, reveal several challenges for such models to serve as useful assistants in the workplace. In addition to the benchmark, we provide a mechanism to effortlessly generate thousands of ground-truth observation/action traces, which can be used for fine-tuning existing models. Overall, we expect this work to serve as a useful resource to help the community progress toward capable autonomous agents. The benchmark can be found at https://github.com/ServiceNow/WorkArena.\n△ Less\nSubmitted 5 February, 2025; v1 submitted 7 July, 2024;\noriginally announced July 2024.","message":"The returned content is the main text content extracted from the page.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:52","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: Control Synthesis Along Uncertain Trajectories Using Integral Quadratic Constraints\nauthor: Biertümpfel; Felix; Seiler; Peter; Pfifer; Harald\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.03101v1\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: The paper presents a novel approach to synthesize robust controllers for nonlinear systems along perturbed trajectories. The approach linearizes the system with respect to a reference trajectory. In contrast to existing methods rooted in robust linear time-varying synthesis, the approach accurately includes perturbations that drive the system away from the reference trajectory. Hence, the controller obtained in the linear framework provides a significantly more robust nonlinear performance. The calculation of the controller is derived from robust synthesis approaches rooted in the integral quadratic constraints framework. The feasibility of the approach is demonstrated on a pitch tracker design for a space launcher.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2025-07-03\nlicense: CC BY-NC-ND 4.0\n---\nElectrical Engineering and Systems Science > Systems and Control\n[Submitted on 3 Jul 2025]\nTitle:Control Synthesis Along Uncertain Trajectories Using Integral Quadratic Constraints\nView PDFAbstract:The paper presents a novel approach to synthesize robust controllers for nonlinear systems along perturbed trajectories. The approach linearizes the system with respect to a reference trajectory. In contrast to existing methods rooted in robust linear time-varying synthesis, the approach accurately includes perturbations that drive the system away from the reference trajectory. Hence, the controller obtained in the linear framework provides a significantly more robust nonlinear performance. The calculation of the controller is derived from robust synthesis approaches rooted in the integral quadratic constraints framework. The feasibility of the approach is demonstrated on a pitch tracker design for a space launcher.\nCurrent browse context:\neess.SY\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nPapers with Code (What is Papers with Code?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:55","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: A Framework for Double-Blind Federated Adaptation of Foundation Models\nauthor: Tastan; Nurbek; Nandakumar; Karthik\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.01289v2\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: Foundation models (FMs) excel in zero-shot tasks but benefit from task-specific adaptation. However, privacy concerns prevent data sharing among multiple data owners, and proprietary restrictions prevent the learning service provider (LSP) from sharing the FM. In this work, we propose BlindFed, a framework enabling collaborative FM adaptation while protecting both parties: data owners do not access the FM or each other's data, and the LSP does not see sensitive task data. BlindFed relies on fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) and consists of three key innovations: (i) FHE-friendly architectural modifications via polynomial approximations and low-rank adapters, (ii) a two-stage split learning approach combining offline knowledge distillation and online encrypted inference for adapter training without backpropagation through the FM, and (iii) a privacy-boosting scheme using sample permutations and stochastic block sampling to mitigate model extraction attacks. Empirical results on four image classification datasets demonstrate the practical feasibility of the BlindFed framework, albeit at a high communication cost and large computational complexity for the LSP.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2025-02-03\nlicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0\n---\nComputer Science > Machine Learning\n[Submitted on 3 Feb 2025 (v1), last revised 1 Oct 2025 (this version, v2)]\nTitle:A Framework for Double-Blind Federated Adaptation of Foundation Models\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:Foundation models (FMs) excel in zero-shot tasks but benefit from task-specific adaptation. However, privacy concerns prevent data sharing among multiple data owners, and proprietary restrictions prevent the learning service provider (LSP) from sharing the FM. In this work, we propose BlindFed, a framework enabling collaborative FM adaptation while protecting both parties: data owners do not access the FM or each other's data, and the LSP does not see sensitive task data. BlindFed relies on fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) and consists of three key innovations: (i) FHE-friendly architectural modifications via polynomial approximations and low-rank adapters, (ii) a two-stage split learning approach combining offline knowledge distillation and online encrypted inference for adapter training without backpropagation through the FM, and (iii) a privacy-boosting scheme using sample permutations and stochastic block sampling to mitigate model extraction attacks. Empirical results on four image classification datasets demonstrate the practical feasibility of the BlindFed framework, albeit at a high communication cost and large computational complexity for the LSP.\nSubmission history\nFrom: Nurbek Tastan [view email][v1] Mon, 3 Feb 2025 12:00:11 UTC (1,910 KB)\n[v2] Wed, 1 Oct 2025 15:14:33 UTC (691 KB)\nCurrent browse context:\ncs.LG\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\nIArxiv Recommender\n(What is IArxiv?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:56","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: Learning to Ask: When LLM Agents Meet Unclear Instruction\nauthor: Wang; Wenxuan; Shi; Juluan; Ling; Zixuan; Chan; Yuk-Kit; Chaozheng; Lee; Cheryl; Yuan; Youliang; Huang; Jen-tse; Jiao; Wenxiang; Lyu; Michael R\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.00557v3\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: Equipped with the capability to call functions, modern large language models (LLMs) can leverage external tools for addressing a range of tasks unattainable through language skills alone. However, the effective execution of these tools relies heavily not just on the advanced capabilities of LLMs but also on precise user instructions, which often cannot be ensured in the real world. To evaluate the performance of LLMs tool-use under imperfect instructions, we meticulously examine the real-world instructions queried from users, analyze the error patterns, and build a challenging tool-use benchmark called Noisy ToolBench (NoisyToolBench). We find that due to the next-token prediction training objective, LLMs tend to arbitrarily generate the missed argument, which may lead to hallucinations and risks. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework, Ask-when-Needed (AwN), which prompts LLMs to ask questions to users whenever they encounter obstacles due to unclear instructions. Moreover, to reduce the manual labor involved in user-LLM interaction and assess LLMs performance in tool utilization from both accuracy and efficiency perspectives, we design an automated evaluation tool named ToolEvaluator. Our experiments demonstrate that the AwN significantly outperforms existing frameworks for tool learning in the NoisyToolBench. We will release all related code and datasets to support future research.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2024-08-31\n---\nComputer Science > Computation and Language\n[Submitted on 31 Aug 2024 (v1), last revised 16 Feb 2025 (this version, v3)]\nTitle:Learning to Ask: When LLM Agents Meet Unclear Instruction\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:Equipped with the capability to call functions, modern large language models (LLMs) can leverage external tools for addressing a range of tasks unattainable through language skills alone. However, the effective execution of these tools relies heavily not just on the advanced capabilities of LLMs but also on precise user instructions, which often cannot be ensured in the real world. To evaluate the performance of LLMs tool-use under imperfect instructions, we meticulously examine the real-world instructions queried from users, analyze the error patterns, and build a challenging tool-use benchmark called Noisy ToolBench (NoisyToolBench). We find that due to the next-token prediction training objective, LLMs tend to arbitrarily generate the missed argument, which may lead to hallucinations and risks. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework, Ask-when-Needed (AwN), which prompts LLMs to ask questions to users whenever they encounter obstacles due to unclear instructions. Moreover, to reduce the manual labor involved in user-LLM interaction and assess LLMs performance in tool utilization from both accuracy and efficiency perspectives, we design an automated evaluation tool named ToolEvaluator. Our experiments demonstrate that the AwN significantly outperforms existing frameworks for tool learning in the NoisyToolBench. We will release all related code and datasets to support future research.\nSubmission history\nFrom: Wenxuan Wang [view email][v1] Sat, 31 Aug 2024 23:06:12 UTC (744 KB)\n[v2] Wed, 4 Sep 2024 20:34:27 UTC (744 KB)\n[v3] Sun, 16 Feb 2025 14:50:40 UTC (750 KB)\nCurrent browse context:\ncs.CL\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nPapers with Code (What is Papers with Code?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:57","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: MP-PINN: A Multi-Phase Physics-Informed Neural Network for Epidemic Forecasting\nauthor: Nguyen; Thang; Dung; Pham; Kha; Tran; Truyen\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.06781v1\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: Forecasting temporal processes such as virus spreading in epidemics often requires more than just observed time-series data, especially at the beginning of a wave when data is limited. Traditional methods employ mechanistic models like the SIR family, which make strong assumptions about the underlying spreading process, often represented as a small set of compact differential equations. Data-driven methods such as deep neural networks make no such assumptions and can capture the generative process in more detail, but fail in long-term forecasting due to data limitations. We propose a new hybrid method called MP-PINN (Multi-Phase Physics-Informed Neural Network) to overcome the limitations of these two major approaches. MP-PINN instils the spreading mechanism into a neural network, enabling the mechanism to update in phases over time, reflecting the dynamics of the epidemics due to policy interventions. Experiments on COVID-19 waves demonstrate that MP-PINN achieves superior performance over pure data-driven or model-driven approaches for both short-term and long-term forecasting.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2024-11-11\nlicense: CC BY 4.0\n---\nComputer Science > Artificial Intelligence\n[Submitted on 11 Nov 2024]\nTitle:MP-PINN: A Multi-Phase Physics-Informed Neural Network for Epidemic Forecasting\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:Forecasting temporal processes such as virus spreading in epidemics often requires more than just observed time-series data, especially at the beginning of a wave when data is limited. Traditional methods employ mechanistic models like the SIR family, which make strong assumptions about the underlying spreading process, often represented as a small set of compact differential equations. Data-driven methods such as deep neural networks make no such assumptions and can capture the generative process in more detail, but fail in long-term forecasting due to data limitations. We propose a new hybrid method called MP-PINN (Multi-Phase Physics-Informed Neural Network) to overcome the limitations of these two major approaches. MP-PINN instils the spreading mechanism into a neural network, enabling the mechanism to update in phases over time, reflecting the dynamics of the epidemics due to policy interventions. Experiments on COVID-19 waves demonstrate that MP-PINN achieves superior performance over pure data-driven or model-driven approaches for both short-term and long-term forecasting.\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nPapers with Code (What is Papers with Code?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? To address this gap, this dissertation extends TheAgentCompany with a finance-focused environment and investigates whether a general purpose LLM agent can complete representative wealth-management tasks both accurately and economically…\n▽ More\nModern work relies on an assortment of digital collaboration tools, yet routine processes continue to suffer from human error and delay. To address this gap, this dissertation extends TheAgentCompany with a finance-focused environment and investigates whether a general purpose LLM agent can complete representative wealth-management tasks both accurately and economically. This study introduces synthetic domain data, enriches colleague simulations, and prototypes an automatic task-generation pipeline. The study aims to create and assess an evaluation set that can meaningfully measure an agent's fitness for assistant-level wealth management work. We construct a benchmark of 12 task-pairs for wealth management assistants spanning retrieval, analysis, and synthesis/communication, with explicit acceptance criteria and deterministic graders. We seeded a set of new finance-specific data and introduced a high vs. low-autonomy variant of every task. The paper concluded that agents are limited less by mathematical reasoning and more so by end-to-end workflow reliability, and meaningfully affected by autonomy level, and that incorrect evaluation of models have hindered benchmarking.\n△ Less\nSubmitted 1 December, 2025;\noriginally announced December 2025.\nTheAgentCompany: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Consequential Real World Tasks\nAuthors:\nFrank F. Xu,\nYufan Song,\nBoxuan Li,\nYuxuan Tang,\nKritanjali Jain,\nMengxue Bao,\nZora Z. Wang,\nXuhui Zhou,\nZhitong Guo,\nMurong Cao,\nMingyang Yang,\nHao Yang Lu,\nAmaad Martin,\nZhe Su,\nLeander Maben,\nRaj Mehta,\nWayne Chi,\nLawrence Jang,\nYiqing Xie,\nShuyan Zhou,\nGraham Neubig\nAbstract:\n…of AI may have on the labor market. To measure the progress of these LLM agents' performance on performing real-world professional tasks, in this paper we introduce TheAgentCompany, an extensible benchmark for evaluating AI agents that interact with the world in similar ways to those of a digital worker: by browsin…\n▽ More\nWe interact with computers on an everyday basis, be it in everyday life or work, and many aspects of work can be done entirely with access to a computer and the Internet. At the same time, thanks to improvements in large language models (LLMs), there has also been a rapid development in AI agents that interact with and affect change in their surrounding environments. But how performant are AI agents at accelerating or even autonomously performing work-related tasks? The answer to this question has important implications both for industry looking to adopt AI into their workflows and for economic policy to understand the effects that adoption of AI may have on the labor market. To measure the progress of these LLM agents' performance on performing real-world professional tasks, in this paper we introduce TheAgentCompany, an extensible benchmark for evaluating AI agents that interact with the world in similar ways to those of a digital worker: by browsing the Web, writing code, running programs, and communicating with other coworkers. We build a self-contained environment with internal web sites and data that mimics a small software company environment, and create a variety of tasks that may be performed by workers in such a company. We test baseline agents powered by both closed API-based and open-weights language models (LMs), and find that the most competitive agent can complete 30% of tasks autonomously. This paints a nuanced picture on task automation with LM agents--in a setting simulating a real workplace, a good portion of simpler tasks could be solved autonomously, but more difficult long-horizon tasks are still beyond the reach of current systems. We release code, data, environment, and experiments on https://the-agent-company.com.\n△ Less\nSubmitted 10 September, 2025; v1 submitted 18 December, 2024;\noriginally announced December 2024.","message":"The returned content is the main text content extracted from the page.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:59","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: Showing 1–1 of 1 results for all: WorkBench Styles workplace agent\nauthor: Authors Olly Styles; Sam Miller; Patricio Cerda-Mardini; Tanaya Guha; Victor Sanchez; Bertie Vidgen\ndate: 2024-05-01\n---\nWorkBench: a Benchmark Dataset for Agents in a Realistic Workplace Setting\nAuthors:\nOlly Styles,\nSam Miller,\nPatricio Cerda-Mardini,\nTanaya Guha,\nVictor Sanchez,\nBertie Vidgen\nAbstract:\nWe introduce WorkBench: a benchmark dataset for evaluating…\n▽ More\nWe introduce WorkBench: a benchmark dataset for evaluating agents' ability to execute tasks in a workplace setting. WorkBench contains a sandbox environment with five databases, 26 tools, and 690 tasks. These tasks represent common business activities, such as sending emails and scheduling meetings. The tasks in WorkBench are challenging as they require planning, tool selection, and often multiple actions. If a task has been successfully executed, one (or more) of the database values may change. The correct outcome for each task is unique and unambiguous, which allows for robust, automated evaluation. We call this key contribution outcome-centric evaluation. We evaluate five existing ReAct agents on WorkBench, finding they successfully complete as few as 3% of tasks (Llama2-70B), and just 43% for the best-performing (GPT-4). We further find that agents' errors can result in the wrong action being taken, such as an email being sent to the wrong person. WorkBench reveals weaknesses in agents' ability to undertake common business activities, raising questions about their use in high-stakes workplace settings. WorkBench is publicly available as a free resource at https://github.com/olly-styles/WorkBench.\n△ Less\nSubmitted 3 August, 2024; v1 submitted 1 May, 2024;\noriginally announced May 2024.","message":"The returned content is the main text content extracted from the page.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

However,\nthree pivotal questions remain unanswered: (1)\nHow effective are current LLMs in utilizing\ntools? (2) How can we enhance LLMs’ ability\nto utilize tools? (3) What obstacles need to be\novercome to leverage tools? To address these\nquestions, we introduce API-Bank, a groundbreaking benchmark, specifically designed for\ntool-augmented LLMs. For the first question,\nwe develop a runnable evaluation system consisting of 73 API tools. We annotate 314\ntool-use dialogues with 753 API calls to assess the existing LLMs’ capabilities in planning, retrieving, and calling APIs. For the second question, we construct a comprehensive\ntraining set containing 1,888 tool-use dialogues\nfrom 2,138 APIs spanning 1,000 distinct domains. Using this dataset, we train Lynx, a\ntool-augmented LLM initialized from Alpaca.\nExperimental results demonstrate that GPT-3.5\nexhibits improved tool utilization compared to\nGPT-3, while GPT-4 excels in planning. However, there is still significant potential for further improvement. Moreover, Lynx surpasses\nAlpaca’s tool utilization performance by more\nthan 26 pts and approaches the effectiveness of\nGPT-3.5. Through error analysis, we highlight\nthe key challenges for future research in this\nfield to answer the third question 1 .\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nOver the past several years, significant progress has\nbeen made in the development of large language\nmodels (LLMs), including GPT-3 (Brown et al.,\n2020), Codex (Chen et al., 2021), ChatGPT, and\nimpressive GPT-4 (Bubeck et al., 2023). These\n∗\n\nEqual Contributions\nCorresponding author.\n1\nThe data and code are publicly available at\nhttps://github.com/AlibabaResearch/DAMO-ConvAI/\ntree/main/api-bank\n†\n\nmodels exhibit increasingly human-like capabilities, such as powerful conversation, in-context\nlearning, and code generation across a wide range\nof open-domain tasks (Bubeck et al., 2023).\nDespite their utility, LLMs are still constrained\nby training data (Brown et al., 2020; Zhao et al.,\n2023; Song et al., 2023). This data can quickly become outdated and may not cover all scenarios (Mialon et al., 2023). Consequently, there has been\na surge of research focused on enhancing LLMs\nby enabling them to leverage external API tools,\nsuch as accessing up-to-date information (Izacard\net al., 2022) and interacting with third-party services (Liang et al., 2023). Traditionally, tool usage\nhas been viewed as a uniquely human behavior,\nwith its emergence considered a significant milestone in primate evolution (Ambrose, 2001). Drawing an analogy to the timeline of human evolution,\nwe argue that it is currently imperative to address\nthree pivotal questions: (1) How effective are current LLMs at utilizing tools? (2) How can we\nenhance LLMs’ ability to utilize tools? (3) What\nobstacles still need to be overcome for LLMs to\neffectively leverage tools?\nTo tackle these inquiries, we present API-Bank,\na groundbreaking benchmark specifically designed\nfor tool-augmented LLMs. In order to determine\nthe users’ needs regarding the utilization of toolaugmented LLMs, we initially conducted interviews with 500 users. Taking into account their\nfeedback, we establish the design principles for\nAPI-Bank. The evaluation scope of API-Bank must\nencompass three essential capabilities: planning,\nretrieving, and calling API tools, in order to fulfill the users’ requirements. Additionally, while\nconstructing the benchmark, it is imperative to consider various aspects such as domain diversity, API\ndiversity, API authenticity, and evaluation authenticity. Subsequently, to answer the first research\nquestion, we implement the evaluation system of\nAPI-Bank, adhering to design principles. The sys-\n\n\ftem comprises 73 commonly used APIs, along with\n314 tool-use dialogues containing 753 API calls,\nall manually annotated, thus forming the first executable and authentic system capable of evaluating\nthe effectiveness of LLM utilizing tools.\nFor the second question, we develop a comprehensive tool-augmented LLM training dataset\nwithin API-Bank. This dataset comprises 2,138\ndistinct APIs and includes 1,888 dialogues with\na total of 4,149 API calls, reflecting three different levels of API usage abilities. However, annotating thousands of APIs and the corresponding dialogues, adhering to our design principles,\nis not only prohibitively expensive but also timeconsuming. In this work, we introduce Multi-agent,\na novel method using LLMs instead of humans to\nautomatically mass-produce tool-augmented LLM\ntraining data. Multi-agent consists of five collaborative agents that step-by-step generate domains,\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

On one hand,\nintegrating tools into LLMs can enhance capabilities in multiple domains, for example, ToolTransformer (Schick et al., 2023) enhances the ability of\nLLMs to solve complex problems by utilizing tools.\nOn the other hand, adopting a tool usage paradigm\ncan improve the robustness of the response and\nthe transparency of the generation, thus increasing\nthe explainability and trust of usersusers (Schick\net al., 2023), as well as improving the system’s\nadaptability. As this field continues to evolve, it is\nessential to comprehensively evaluate all aspects\nof tool usage, particularly in complex scenarios.\n\nLarge Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated significant potential in decision-making\nand reasoning, particularly when integrated\nwith various tools to effectively solve complex problems. However, existing benchmarks\nfor evaluating LLMs’ tool usage face several\nlimitations: (1) limited evaluation scenarios,\noften lacking assessments in real multi-turn dialogue contexts; (2) narrow evaluation dimensions, with insufficient detailed assessments of\nhow LLMs use tools; and (3) reliance on LLMs\nor real API executions for evaluation, which\nintroduces significant overhead. To address\nthese challenges, we introduce ACEBench, a\ncomprehensive benchmark for assessing tool\nusage in LLMs. ACEBench categorizes data\ninto three primary types based on evaluation\nmethodology: Normal, Special, and Agent.\n\"Normal\" evaluates tool usage in basic scenarios; \"Special\" evaluates tool usage in situations with ambiguous or incomplete instructions; \"Agent\" evaluates tool usage through\nmulti-agent interactions to simulate real-world,\nmulti-turn dialogues. We conducted extensive\nexperiments using ACEBench, analyzing various LLMs in-depth and providing a more granular examination of error causes across different data types.\n\n1\n\nWhile several studies have focused on evaluating\ntool usage (Yan et al., 2024; Guo et al., 2024; Wang\net al., 2024a; Qin et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024b;\nZhuang et al., 2023; Lu et al., 2024), there are still\nsome shortcomings in the existing tool-use benchmarks. Firstly, existing benchmarks lack multi-turn\ndialogue evaluation in real-world scenarios. For\nexample, the multi-turn dialogues in BFCL (Yan\net al., 2024) and HammerBench (Wang et al.,\n2024a) are composed of predefined fixed content\ncombinations. Secondly, current tool-use benchmarks (Qin et al., 2023; Guo et al., 2024; Huang\net al., 2023; Li et al., 2023) lack fine-grained evaluation and personalized data assessment. Additionally, existing benchmarks (Qin et al., 2023;\nGuo et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024b) ignore the\nassessment of special cases, or the evaluation methods are simplistic (Yan et al., 2024), as user instructions in real life are not always perfect(Wang\net al., 2024c). The model’s ability to recognize and\nhandle these issues is also crucial for evaluation.\nLastly, evaluation costs are high (Qin et al., 2023;\nGuo et al., 2024), as many studies rely on advanced\nlarge models for evaluation.\n\nIntroduction\n\nLarge Language Models (LLMs), such as GPT4 (Achiam et al., 2023), have demonstrated exceptional performance across numerous natural language processing tasks (Naveed et al., 2023; Qu\net al., 2025; Mialon et al., 2023). Studies have\nshown that incorporating tools can significantly expand LLM capabilities, particularly in specialized\ndomains such as mathematics (Das et al., 2024; Bulusu et al., 2024; Gou et al., 2023; Veerendranath\n†\nEqual Contributions. Work was done during an internship\nat Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab. * Corresponding authors.\n*\nThe code are already publicly available at GitHub.\n\nTo address these shortcomings, we propose\n1\n\n\fTable 1: Comparison of benchmarks across different evaluation criteria. \"LLM-Free\" refers to result evaluation\nwithout relying on LLMs. \"Robustness\" refers to incomplete or unclear user instructions. \"Interactiveness\" refers\nto the dynamic interaction between the model and the environment. \"Atomic-Level\" refers to analyzing from the\natomic-level capabilities. \"Personalization” refers to the inclusion of personal likes.\nBenchmark\nMetaTool (Huang et al., 2023)\nAPI-Bank (Li et al., 2023)\nStable ToolBench (Guo et al., 2024)\nBFCL (Yan et al., 2024)\nτ -Bench (Yao et al., 2024)\nHammerBench (Wang et al., 2024a)\nACEBench (Ours)\n\nLLM-Free\n\nRobustness\n\nInteractiveness\n\nAtomic-Level\n\nPersonalization\n\n✓\n✓\n✗\n✓\n✓\n✗\n✓\n\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✓\n✗\n✓\n✓\n\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✓\n✗\n✓\n\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✓\n\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✓\n\nACEBench, a comprehensive tool-use benchmark\nthat includes the following categories:\nNormal. Consists of fixed question-answer pairs\nand encompasses a variety of scenarios, including\nsingle-turn dialogues, multi-turn dialogues, and\npersonalized scenario data. It also includes evaluations of atomic-level capabilities.\nSpecial. Includes imperfect instructions, such as\ninstructions containing incomplete parameters, incorrectly formatted parameters, or questions irrelevant to the capabilities of the candidate functions.\nAgent. Encompasses real-world scenarios, abstracted to construct multi-turn, multi-step tool\ninvocation scenarios, divided into multi-turn and\nmulti-step cases depending on whether the user\nparticipates in the dialogue process.\nThe three categories above cover most of the tool\nusage scenarios for LLMs, and detailed explanations of each category can be found in Appendix A.\nOur main contributions are as follows:\n\nevaluation of LLMs’ tool usage.\n\n2\n\nRelated Works\n\nThe emerging trend of leveraging LLMs’ tool-use\ncapabilities in real-world applications underscores\nthe need for comprehensive evaluations of their\nperformance and effectiveness. Despite recent advancements, existing benchmarks for evaluating\nthe tool-use capabilities of LLMs still have significant limitations\nStable ToolBench (Guo et al., 2024) addresses\nthe issue of unstable external APIs by employing\na virtual API server, but its dependence on large\nmodels for evaluation results in high costs and\nscalability challenges. BFCL (Yan et al., 2024) introduces a benchmark for tool use in multi-turn dialogue scenarios. Yet, it assembles dialogues from\nfixed content, failing to capture the dynamic and\nadaptive nature of real-world interactions. Similarly, τ -Bench (Yao et al., 2024) evaluates language\nagents’ ability to engage with human users while\nadhering to domain-specific rules. Still, its narrow\nfocus on just two scenarios limits its generalizability across diverse tasks. HammerBench (Wang\net al., 2024a) improves upon this by incorporating datasets derived from popular mobile applications and merging dialogues to simulate typical\nquestion-answer trajectories. However, like BFCL,\nits multi-turn dialogues are simplistic concatenations of pre-defined content, which do not reflect\nthe complexities of real-world conversational dynamics. In addition, some benchmarks (Qin et al.,\n2023; Guo et al., 2024) rely on large language models (LLMs) for result evaluation, leading to high\ncosts and unstable operations.\nIn contrast, our work addresses these limitations\nby expanding the scope of evaluation to encompass a broader range of tool usage scenarios. We\n\n• Comprehensive Benchmark Evaluation. We\npropose a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLMs’ tool usage, covering various scenarios, including more fine-grained evaluation\nperspectives and assessments under imperfect instructions and providing more stable evaluation\nmetrics.\n• Sandbox Environment and Automated Evaluation System. We build an end-to-end automated evaluation system and develop a sandbox\nenvironment construction scheme for multi-turn,\nmulti-step tool invocation based on real-world\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

We define the \\emph{single-deletion single-substitution ball} function $ \\mathcal{B} $ as a mapping from a sequence to the set of sequences that can be derived from it by performing one deletion and one substitution. A binary \\emph{$(n,N;\\mathcal{B})$-reconstruction code} is defined as a collection of binary sequences of length $ n $ such that the intersection size between the single-deletion single-substitution balls of any two distinct codewords is strictly less than $ N $. This property ensures that each codeword can be uniquely reconstructed from $ N $ distinct elements in its single-deletion single-substitution ball. Our main contribution is to demonstrate that when $ N $ is set to $ 4n - 8 $, $ 3n - 4 $, $2n+9$, $ n+21 $, $31$, and $7$, the redundancy of binary $(n,N;\\mathcal{B})$-reconstruction codes can be $0$, $1$, $2$, $ \\log\\log n + 3 $, $\\log n + 1 $, and $ 3\\log n + 4 $, respectively, where the logarithm is on base two.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2025-05-07\n---\nComputer Science > Information Theory\n[Submitted on 7 May 2025]\nTitle:Binary Reconstruction Codes for Correcting One Deletion and One Substitution\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:In this paper, we investigate binary reconstruction codes capable of correcting one deletion and one substitution. We define the \\emph{single-deletion single-substitution ball} function $ \\mathcal{B} $ as a mapping from a sequence to the set of sequences that can be derived from it by performing one deletion and one substitution. A binary \\emph{$(n,N;\\mathcal{B})$-reconstruction code} is defined as a collection of binary sequences of length $ n $ such that the intersection size between the single-deletion single-substitution balls of any two distinct codewords is strictly less than $ N $. Turkkan,\nGerard Vanloo,\nMichael Nidd,\nTing Dai,\nOishik Chatterjee,\nPranjal Gupta,\nSuranjana Samanta,\nPooja Aggarwal,\nRong Lee,\nPavankumar Murali\n, et al. (18 additional authors not shown)\nAbstract:\nRealizing the vision of using AI…\n▽ More\nRealizing the vision of using AI agents to automate critical IT tasks depends on the ability to measure and understand effectiveness of proposed solutions. We introduce ITBench, a framework that offers a systematic methodology for benchmarking AI agents to address real-world IT automation tasks. Our initial release targets three key areas: Site Reliability Engineering (SRE), Compliance and Security Operations (CISO), and Financial Operations (FinOps). The design enables AI researchers to understand the challenges and opportunities of AI agents for IT automation with push-button workflows and interpretable metrics. ITBench includes an initial set of 94 real-world scenarios, which can be easily extended by community contributions. Our results show that agents powered by state-of-the-art models resolve only 13.8% of SRE scenarios, 25.2% of CISO scenarios, and 0% of FinOps scenarios. We expect ITBench to be a key enabler of AI-driven IT automation that is correct, safe, and fast.\n△ Less\nSubmitted 7 February, 2025;\noriginally announced February 2025.","message":"The returned content is the main text content extracted from the page.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:88","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"τ -bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User\nInteraction in Real-World Domains\nShunyu Yao∗\n\nNoah Shinn\n\nPedram Razavi\n\nKarthik Narasimhan\n\narXiv:2406.12045v1 [cs.AI] 17 Jun 2024\n\nSierra\n\nAbstract\nExisting benchmarks do not test language agents on their interaction with human\nusers or ability to follow domain-specific rules, both of which are vital for deploying\nthem in real world applications. We propose τ -bench, a benchmark emulating\ndynamic conversations between a user (simulated by language models) and a\nlanguage agent provided with domain-specific API tools and policy guidelines.\nWe employ an efficient and faithful evaluation process that compares the database\nstate at the end of a conversation with the annotated goal state. We also propose\na new metric (pass^k) to evaluate the reliability of agent behavior over multiple\ntrials. Our experiments show that even state-of-the-art function calling agents (like\ngpt-4o) succeed on < 50% of the tasks, and are quite inconsistent (pass^8 < 25%\nin retail). Our findings point to the need for methods that can improve the ability\nof agents to act consistently and follow rules reliably.\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nThere is increasing excitement around the potential of language agents [20, 24, 18, 1] to enable new\nlevels of automation across various industries. However, their deployment in real-world systems\nrequires several key desiderata to be satisfied. Agents must (1) interact seamlessly with both humans\nand programmatic APIs over long horizons to incrementally gather information and resolve intents,\n(2) accurately adhere to complex policies and rules specific to a task or domain, and (3) maintain\nconsistency and reliability at scale, across millions of interactions. For instance, consider the case of\nan airline booking agent (Figure 1). When a user wants to change their flight reservation to a different\ndestination airport, the agent needs to gather the required information by interacting with the user,\ncheck the airline policies using the guidelines provided, and find new flights and (if possible) rebook\nthe user using complex reservation APIs. In addition, the agent should be consistent in its behavior\nacross different kinds of users with the same request, and robust to small changes in the conversation\nflow that should not affect the end outcome.\nModeling realistic human interaction and rule following in agent evaluation is vital for developing\nand deploying trustworthy agents in the wild, and tackling challenges like long-context reasoning\nand planning in a methodical fashion. Existing benchmarks [27, 29, 12, 14, 16] for language agents\noften feature simplified instruction-following setups, where the agent autonomously interacts with an\nenvironment (web, code terminal, or APIs) given all the information upfront, without any human-inthe-loop interaction and without the need to consult any domain-specific guidelines.\nIn this work, we introduce τ -bench (short for Tool-Agent-User Interaction Benchmark) to measure\nan agent’s ability to interact with (simulated) human users and programmatic APIs while following\ndomain-specific policies in a consistent manner. τ -bench is built in a modular framework with\n(1) realistic databases and APIs, (2) domain-specific policy documents, and (3) instructions for\n∗\n\nWork done during internship. Code and data: https://github.com/sierra-research/tau-bench.\n\nPreprint. Under review.\n\n\f(a) τ-bench setup\n\nTools\n\n(b) Example trajectory in τ-airline\n\nget_user_details\n\nbook_reservation\n\ncancel_reservation\n\nupdate_reservation_flights\n\nChange flight\n……\n\n……\n\nAgent\n\nDomain policy as system prompt\nCurrent time is 2024-5-15 15:00:00 EST.\n- Basic economy cannot be modified.\n- Basic economy cannot be cancelled after 24 hours\nof booking… (more rules omitted)\n\nUser\n\nUser instruction as system prompt\nYou are mia_li_2017, and want to change the your\nmost recent reservation to fly to SF instead of LA on\nthe same day. The benchmark tests an agent’s ability to collate and convey all required information\nfrom/to users through multiple interactions, and solve complex issues on the fly while ensuring it\nfollows guidelines laid out in a domain-specific policy document. (b) An example trajectory in\nτ -airline, where an agent needs to reject the user request (change a basic economy flight) following\ndomain policies and propose a new solution (cancel and rebook). This challenges the agent in\nlong-context zero-shot reasoning over complex databases, rules, and user intents.\ndiverse user scenarios and corresponding ground truth annotations. As a first demonstration, we\nfocus on the realm of customer service and create two different domains where agents need to assist\nsimulated users with diverse requests (τ -retail and τ -airline). We leverage the generative capabilities\nof language models (LMs) for data creation and realistic human user simulation [15] in conjunction\nwith manual annotation and verification.\nWe constructed τ -bench in three stages, including manual schema and API design, LM-assisted\ngeneration of data entries, and manual scenario generation and verification for the user simulator.\nOur evaluation scheme compares the database state at the end of each episode with the ground truth\nexpected state. This allows for objective measurement of the agent’s decision making, while also\nproviding room for stochastic variation in the conversation itself, since the user may pose the same\nrequest in different ways that result in the same end state of the database. We also introduce the\nmetric of pass^k, which measures the consistency and robustness of the agent across k i.i.d. trials.\nOur experiments reveal that agents built with simple LM constructs (like function calling or ReAct)\nperform poorly, highlighting the need for more sophisticated agent architectures. For instance, even\nstate-of-the-art LMs like gpt-4o achieve low task success rates (pass^1) using function calling\n(∼61% on τ -retail and ∼35% on τ -airline). With increasing k, the chance of consistently solving a\ntask drops rapidly, to as low as ∼25% for pass^8 on τ -retail for the same model. This showcases\nthe fragile nature of such agents in handling stochasticity and partial information, which is common\nin human-agent interaction. Upon analyzing the failure cases, we find that current agents struggle\nwith complex reasoning over databases, understanding and following ad-hoc policies, and handling\ncompound (more than one) requests. We hope that τ -bench enables the evaluation and development\nof more consistent and capable agents for real-world digital tasks involving human interaction.\n\n2\n\nRelated Work\n\nMost existing benchmarks for agents and task-oriented dialogue systems focus on evaluating either\nconversational or tool-use capabilities. τ -bench aims to unify both under realistic settings, while also\ntesting how well agents can follow domain-specific policies in a consistent manner.\nBenchmarks for language agents and tool use. Several benchmarks have been developed to\nevaluate agents powered by LMs [27, 29, 12, 14, 16] . Recent efforts have focused specifically on\nevaluating tool use capabilities of LMs, i.e., their ability to generate the right function calls from a\nset of functions in an API.
