{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:89","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"τ 2-Bench: Evaluating Conversational Agents in a\nDual-Control Environment\n\narXiv:2506.07982v1 [cs.AI] 9 Jun 2025\n\nVictor Barres ∗\nSierra\nvictor@sierra.ai\n\nHonghua Dong ∗ †\nSierra & University of Toronto & Vector Institute\nhonghuad@cs.toronto.edu\n\nXujie Si\nUniversity of Toronto & Vector Institute\nsix@cs.toronto.edu\n\nSoham Ray\nSierra\nsoham@sierra.ai\n\nKarthik Narasimhan\nSierra\nkarthik@sierra.ai\n\nAbstract\nExisting benchmarks for conversational AI agents simulate single-control environments, where only the AI agent can use tools to interact with the world, while the\nuser remains a passive information provider. This differs from real-world scenarios\nlike technical support, where users need to actively participate in modifying the\nstate of the (shared) world. In order to address this gap, we introduce τ 2 -bench,\nwith four key contributions: 1) A novel Telecom dual-control domain modeled as\na Dec-POMDP, where both agent and user make use of tools to act in a shared,\ndynamic environment that tests both agent coordination and communication, 2)\nA compositional task generator that programmatically creates diverse, verifiable\ntasks from atomic components, ensuring domain coverage and controlled complexity, 3) A reliable user simulator tightly coupled with the environment, whose\nbehavior is constrained by tools and observable states, improving simulation fidelity, 4) fine-grained analysis of agent performance through multiple ablations\nincluding separating errors arising from reasoning vs communication/coordination.\nIn particular, our experiments show significant performance drops when agents\nshift from no-user to dual-control, highlighting the challenges of guiding users.\nOverall, τ 2 -bench provides a controlled testbed for agents that must both reason\neffectively and guide user actions.1\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nExisting benchmarks for conversational AI agents are designed to test their abilities to communicate\neffectively with a user and perform the right sequence of actions to solve tasks [26, 14, 23, 19]. These\nbenchmarks are inherently single-control environments, where the AI agent is able to interact with\nthe world but the (simulated) user is limited to providing information about preferences and goals.\nIn τ -bench [26] for example, the retail and airline domains test the agent’s ability to solve\nconstraint satisfaction tasks, where constraints stem from a combination of a domain policy that the\nagent must follow and the user’s cognitive state including beliefs, goals and preferences.\nIn such settings, the user’s understanding of the agent’s environment (including the set of actions\nthe agent can take) is provided through carefully crafted natural language instructions that help\nensure a single, solvable path for the constraint satisfaction problem. The agent have access to a set of tools\nthat interact with a database, and is tasked with resolving the user’s request via Tool-Agent-User\n(TAU) interactions while adhering to the domain policy. To test real-world scenarios, the user is\nsimulated by another AI agent given a scenario-based instruction and a set of tools that interact with\nits own database. The simulated user can be regarded as handling an easier version of the TAU\ninteraction in a dual format (Tool-User-Agent), where it only need to follow instructions but does not\nneed to reason about solutions for the task.\n\nspecification, the user can only perceive the agent’s actions through communication and reason about\nthe environment state solely based on the initial instructions. This is quite different from real-world\nscenarios like technical customer support, where the user has to actively participate in taking some\nactions to diagnose and solve problems when asked to do so, such as restarting their phone or turning\noff airplane mode.\nIn order to capture this additional real-world complexity, we introduce τ 2 -bench with a dual-control\nenvironment, where (LLM-simulated) users can take actions and call tools in addition to communicating with the agent. The dual-control model provides a few advantages in improving the user\nsimulation, such as selective information hiding, non-verbal manipulation of the environment, and\neasier specification of the user’s task or scenario, especially in cases where the structured format of\ntools is preferred to unstructured natural language.\nThe primary challenge in a dual-control setup lies in granting the user simulator meaningful agency\nvia tools while maintaining the \"complexity asymmetry\" between the agent and the user simulator –\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Laradji 1\nManuel Del Verme 2 4 Tom Marty 1 2 5 Léo Boisvert 1 3 Megh Thakkar 1 2 5 Quentin Cappart 2 3\nDavid Vazquez 1 Nicolas Chapados 1 2 3 Alexandre Lacoste † 1\n\nAbstract\n\nexperience would involve automated assistants that can\nstreamline these tasks ensuring accessibility for everyone.\nWhile Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) have\nfacilitated programmatic interactions with software, the\nresulting automated assistants often lack transparency, are\ndifficult for users to inspect, and are not universally available.\nIn contrast, assistants that directly manipulate UIs (UI\nassistants) offer greater transparency and are more amenable\nto human oversight. Most notably, because the user can give\nand take back control over the UI at any point, UI assistants\ncan provide varying levels of automation ranging from\npartial assistance (such as finding a menu or filling a form) to\ncomplete task execution (like placing an order), akin to the\nsix levels of automation in autonomous driving (SAE, 2021).\n\nWe study the use of large language model-based\nagents for interacting with software via web\nbrowsers. Unlike prior work, we focus on measuring the agents’ ability to perform tasks that span\nthe typical daily work of knowledge workers utilizing enterprise software systems. To this end, we\npropose WorkArena, a remote-hosted benchmark\nof 33 tasks based on the widely-used ServiceNow\nplatform. We also introduce BrowserGym, an\nenvironment for the design and evaluation of such\nagents, offering a rich set of actions as well as multimodal observations. Our empirical evaluation\nreveals that while current agents show promise\non WorkArena, there remains a considerable gap\ntowards achieving full task automation. Notably,\nour analysis uncovers a significant performance\ndisparity between open and closed-source LLMs,\nhighlighting a critical area for future exploration\nand development in the field.\n\nRecent advancements in the fields of large language and vision models have seen the rapid development of UI assistants,\nparticularly web agents acting through a browser (Furuta\net al., 2023; Kim et al., 2023; Gur et al., 2023b). The range\nof web tasks explored in the literature varies from simple\nUI commands such as selecting specific menu elements on\ntoy web pages (Liu et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2017a), to more\ncomplex requests such as “Checkout merge requests assigned\nto me”, on real-world websites like Reddit and GitLab (Zhou\net al., 2023). Yet, one area in which web agents can be particularly impactful and remain unexplored is enterprise software.\nIn the workplace, where repetitive tasks are common,\nenterprise software often prioritizes functionality over user\nexperience, leading to inefficiencies and long learning curves\nfor workers. Our work addresses this gap, and investigates\nthe potential of web agents in enterprise settings to improve\naccessibility, user experience, and worker productivity.\n\n1. Introduction\nGraphical User Interfaces (UIs) are the predominant medium\nthrough which people interact with software, serving as\na crucial gateway to the digital world. While they have\nevolved to become more intuitive, featuring generic and\nuniversal components like forms, lists, and buttons, UIs\ncan still make complex or repetitive tasks burdensome\nfor users. While being more and more intuitive, those\ncomplex UIs also became inadvertedly more and more\ndiscriminative for visually-impaired users. An ideal user\n\nTo this end, we introduce WorkArena, a benchmark developed on the widely-used ServiceNow platform (ServiceNow,\n2023). ServiceNow is a comprehensive cloud-based platform\nthat offers solutions for automating and managing digital\nworkflows across various enterprise functions, including IT\nservice management, human resources, customer service,\nand security operations. In 2023 their customer base counted\nover 7,000 companies worldwide, including 85% of the\nFortune 500 companies (Mastantuono, 2023). ServiceNow’s extensive\nreach makes it an ideal real-world environment for evaluating\nthe potential impact of UI assistants in the workplace.\n\ncollection of 125 toy web tasks ranging from clicking a specific button to using a basic text editor, and WebShop (Yao\net al., 2022), a simulated e-commerce website with shopping\ntasks that require searching and browsing a catalog of items.\nMore recently, Zhou et al. (2023) introduced WebArena, a collection of 190 tasks based on realistic websites that emulate\nreal-world domains such as e-commerce, social forums, collaborative software development, and content management.\nWebArena is a notoriously challenging benchmark, with a\nsuccess rate of 14% for a state-of-the-art web agent based\non GPT-4, and 78% for human agents. Deng et al. (2023)\nproposed Min2Web, a large-scale dataset of 2,000 web interactions from 137 websites curated by human annotators. Similarly, Lù et al. (2024) propose WebLINX, a curated dataset\nof web interactions composed of 2337 expert demonstrations\nfrom 155 different real-world websites. In WebLINX, each\ntask is composed of a turn-based chat dialogue averaging 43\ninteractions per task. Recently, He et al. (2024) propose 300\ninformation-retrieval tasks, from 15 real-world consumer\nwebsites (e.g., Amazon, Coursera, Booking), which are used\nto evaluate WebVoyager, a vision-based web agent.\n\nOur contributions are as follows:\n• WorkArena: A realistic benchmark of enterprise-related\ntasks for web agents comprising 19,912 unique task\ninstances (§3, Figure 1a);\n• BrowserGym: A new framework for the development and\nevaluation of web agents, compatible with previous benchmarks like WebArena (Zhou et al., 2023), MiniWoB (Liu\net al., 2018; Shi et al., 2017a) and WebShop (Yao et al.,\n2022), that offers a richer set of multimodal observations\n(e.g., screenshot, accessibility tree, screen coordinates),\na broader set of actions (e.g., Python code and high-level\nprimitives), and supports chat-based interactions (§ 4,\nFigure 1b); Surprisingly, these features contribute to\nbringing our GPT-4 agent at the top of the leaderboard\non WebArena, with a score of 25.4%, contrasting with the\nscore of the original paper, 14.4%.\n• Empirical study: We report a collection of experiments\nto assess the ability of state-of-the-art large language\nmodel (LLM)-based agents to solve WorkArena, as well\nas an analysis of the impact of the different BrowserGym\nfeatures on WorkArena and MiniWoB (§5).\n\nWorth mentioning is the related body of work on mobile\nUI agent benchmarks, which includes the early PixelHelp\ndataset (Li et al., 2020) and more recent Android in the Wild\n(Rawles et al., 2023) and Macro Mining (Huang et al., 2024).\nOur proposed benchmark, WorkArena, is designed to\ncomplement existing work by specifically focusing on\nreal-world enterprise software applications. It includes a\nwide range of tasks that collectively encompass several\nend-to-end workflows typically performed by knowledge\nworkers. Additionally, it poses a series of technical\nchallenges, such as pages with very large document object\nmodels (DOMs), non-standard HTML, and complex UI\nelements, which we outline in § 3.2. This benchmark\nintegrates into BrowserGym, a new environment that we\n\n2. X, JULY 2024\n\n1\n\nKGpose: Keypoint-Graph Driven End-to-End\nMulti-Object 6D Pose Estimation via Point-Wise\nPose Voting\n\narXiv:2407.08909v1 [cs.CV] 12 Jul 2024\n\nAndrew Jeong, and Seokhwan Jeong*\n\nAbstract—This letter presents KGpose, a novel end-to-end\nframework for 6D pose estimation of multiple objects. Our\napproach combines keypoint-based method with learnable pose\nregression through ‘keypoint-graph’, which is a graph representation of the keypoints. KGpose first estimates 3D keypoints for\neach object using an attentional multi-modal feature fusion of\nRGB and point cloud features. These keypoints are estimated\nfrom each point of point cloud and converted into a graph representation. The network directly regresses 6D pose parameters\nfor each point through a sequence of keypoint-graph embedding\nand local graph embedding which are designed with graph\nconvolutions, followed by rotation and translation heads. The\nfinal pose for each object is selected from the candidates of pointwise predictions. The method achieves competitive results on the\nbenchmark dataset, demonstrating the effectiveness of our model.\nKGpose enables multi-object pose estimation without requiring\nan extra localization step, offering a unified and efficient solution\nfor understanding geometric contexts in complex scenes for\nrobotic applications.\nIndex Terms—Deep learning, Object pose estimation, End-toend network\n\nI. I NTRODUCTION\n\nW\n\nITH advances in robot technologies, robots are gradually being utilized in industries and in our lives also.\nIn order for robots to accomplish physical tasks effectively\nand safely along with humans, robots should understand the\ngeometric relationships with their surroundings. One approach\nto understand it is to estimate 6D pose, which stands for 3D\nrotation and 3D translation, for each object in the scene and\nthis information is crucial for robotic manipulation or grasping.\nCommon approach for recovering 6D pose of an object\nis to regress 6D pose from keypoints of each object in\nthe scene. Keypoint-based methods usually consist of two\nstages; first, estimate keypoints which could be considered\nas geometric clues for predicting object pose, then predict\n6D pose parameters leveraging the estimated keypoints. Some\nmethods detect 2D locations of the keypoints for each object\nand estimate 6D pose through RANSAC (RANdom SAmple\nConsensus) based Perspective-n-Point (PnP) algorithm establishing 2D-3D correspondences between the keypoints in the\nimage and the ones from 3D object model [1]–[3]. Other\nmethods detect 3D keypoints rather than 2D ones. Estimating\n3D keypoints could be preferred to avoid errors occurred\nfrom 2D projection, such as a reprojection error, a chance\nof overlapping of 2D projections from different keypoints\nAndrew Jeong, and Seokhwan Jeong are with Department of Mechanical\nEngineering, Sogang University, Seoul, South Korea .\n\nFig. 1: Our approach. (a) Each point in input point cloud votes for 3D\nkeypoints of each object in the scene. (b) Each set of the keypoints are\nconverted into a graph, which is called ‘keypoint-graph’. (c) 6D pose of an\nobject is estimated (or voted) from each point through several layers of graph\nconvolution.\n\nin 3D space, or losing geometric constraints for rigid 3D\nobjects [4]–[7]. These methods usually detect 3D keypoints\nadopting a voting scheme [8], and determine 6D pose through\nleast-squares fitting algorithm [9], [10] given the detected 3D\nkeypoints in camera coordinate system and corresponding 3D\nkeypoints in the object coordinate system.\nRecent pose estimation researches have focused on directly\nregressing 6D pose by designing fully differentiable models,\nwhich is referred to as an end-to-end pose estimation [11]–\n[18]. There have been some methods that make RANSACPnP be differentiable [13], [14] to achieve end-to-end learning,\nwhile other methods try to regress the 6D pose directly from\n2D-3D or 3D-3D correspondences [15]–[18]. The output of\nthe model could be 3D rigid transformation matrix [17], [18],\nEuler angles with translation offsets [12], or quaternions [11].\nWe propose an end-to-end object 6D pose estimation framework that combines keypoint-based method with learnable\npose regression module. While most of keypoint-based 6D\npose estimation studies perform keypoint estimation, followed\nby pose regression based on the estimated keypoints, which is\nwhether differentiable or not, we introduce a voting scheme for\n6D pose estimation that generates point-wise pose prediction\nfrom input point cloud and learn to aggregate the candidates of\n6D pose parameters to select the best one. We leveraged graph\nconvolution on ‘keypoint-graphs’, which are constructed by\nconverting estimated 3D keypoints into a graph representation,\nfollowed by disentangled 3D rotation and 3D translation head\nto regress the pose parameters.\n\n\f2\n\nJOURNAL OF LATEX CLASS FILES, VOL. XX, NO. X, JULY 2024\n\nFig. 2: Overview of KGpose. Given RGB-D image, 3D keypoints are estimated through RGB and point cloud branches along with feature fusion process.\nThen, the estimated keypoints and corresponding keypoints from object model are converted into a graph representation and concatenated. These graphs are\npassed through keypoint graph embedding and several layers of local graph embedding stages to embed the graph features into each node (or point). Finally\nthe embedded point features are fed into rotation and translation head to regress 6D pose parameters for each point, which are candidates of 6D pose. The\nfinal 6D pose for each object is determined by selecting the nearest candidate to the mean of the candidates of the object.\n\nII. O UR APPROACH\nWe propose a framework called KGpose that estimates\n3D keypoints of each object and leverages the keypoints as\nintermediate features of given RGB-D image to regress 6D\npose directly in an end-to-end manner. Hence, we will describe\na process of 3D keypoint estimation, followed by pose regression method through graph convolution on ‘keypoint-graphs’\nwhich are graph representations of the estimated keypoints,\nwith all processes being differentiable to enable end-to-end\ntraining for 6D pose estimation.\nA. 3D Keypoint Estimation\n1) Embedding Multi-modal Features: Given an RGB-D\nimage, we designed overall architecture for feature extraction\nas an encoder-decoder structure and extracted appearance\nfeatures from RGB branch, ResNet34 [19] and geometric\nfeatures from point cloud branch, RandLA-Net [20], and\nfused the features from both modalities to learn correlation\nbetween them, following prior works [4], [5], [7]. Our KGpose\nperforms segmentation and keypoint estimation on input point\ncloud, without center point estimation as PVN3D [4] and\nFFB6D [5] did.\nWe adopted attention mechanism along with bidirectional\nfeature fusion [5] which introduced point-to-pixel features\nand pixel-to-point features to exploit context about the other\nbranch of different modality to increase representative power\nof a model. In order for each modality to understand another\nmodality well (e.g., RGB and point cloud), we introduced\nattention mechanism to the feature fusion and skip connection process. There are candidate attention mechanisms to\nutilize for attentional feature fusion on each layer in RGB\nbranch and point cloud branch: from conventional Squeeze and\nExcitation (SE) module [21], Convolutional Block Attention\nModule (CBAM) [22] to transformer based methods like Point\nTransformer [23], Cross ViT [24], or Point-BERT [25]. Among\nthem, we adopted CBAM module for both feature fusion and\nlong / short skip connections, in that it has lightweight design\n\nbut comparable performance to expensive self-attention based\ntransformer architectures in terms of attentive power on 3D\npoint cloud [26]. Also, it is maneuverable so that designing\nspatial and channel attention module for point cloud was\nsimple even though the original architecture was designed for\n2D images.\nAttention module was defined as follows:\nX ′ = M ish(X + Att(X, Y ))\n(1)\n\n\nAtt(X, Y ) = SA(CA(Conv(X ⊕ Y )))\nwhere CA(X) = σ(F (Avg(X)) + F (M ax(X))) ⊙ X\n\n\nSA(X) = σ(Conv(Avg(X) ⊕ M ax(X))) ⊙ X\nwhere Att denotes sequential channel/spatial attention module, CA and SA are channel/spatial attention, Avg and M ax\nare average pooling and max pooling respectively, F refers\nto MLP layers, BN is batch normalization, M ish is Mish\nactivation [27], σ is sigmoid and ⊕ denotes concatenation.\nBoth input features X and Y are first concatenated, passed\nthrough convolution layers, and applied channel attention\nfollowed by spatial attention to generate mutual relationship\nbetween X and Y . Then, feature X is updated by Mish\nactivation following a residual connection.\n\nFig. 3: Revised CBAM module for both feature fusion and skip connections.\n\n\fSHELL et al.: BARE DEMO OF IEEETRAN.CLS FOR IEEE JOURNALS\n\n3\n\nFig. 4: Process of graph embedding. (a) Estimated keypoints are regarded as vertices and edges are defined as vectors from the keypoints to their center.\nEdge features from each graph are passed through Edge Convolution to embed the information about the keypoints to each point that voted for them. (b)\nThe embedded point features construct local graphs about their k-NN and the edge features from the graphs are also fed into Edge Convolution layer to\nembed graph features (c) The point features are sampled and construct local graphs about k-NN to understand the features with a larger receptive field (d)\nThe outcomes from (b) and (c) are fused to update the point features\n\n2) Attentional Bidirectional Feature Fusion: We introduced\nspatial/channel attention to the bidirectional feature fusion.\nOnce the point-to-pixel feature or the pixel-to-point feature\nare produced following [5], these features are fused with RGB\nfeature or point cloud feature with attentional bidirectional\nfeature fusion, then the outcomes from both modalities are\nconcatenated to produce a fused feature (we would call it\n‘RGB-D feature’), which is different from [5], [7]. The RGBD feature was concatenated at each layer of the architecture\ndeliberately to facilitate desirable feature extraction on the last\nRGB-D feature in that segmentation and keypoint estimation\nare performed on the feature concatenated from the RGB\nfeature and point cloud feature from the last layer in decoder.\nReferring to (1), X, Y are RGB feature and point-to-pixel\nfeature when transferring a context of point cloud feature to\nRGB branch, and point cloud feature and pixel-to-point feature\nwhen transferring RGB context to point cloud branch.\n3) Attentional Skip-Connection between Fused Features:\nEach fused feature is derived from the RGB and point cloud\nfeatures of their respective branches. To effectively capture and\nunderstand the fused RGB-D features across different layers,\nwe also incorporated an attention mechanism within the skip\nconnections.\nAs mentioned before, long / short skip connections between\nfused RGB-D features were also designed with attention\nmodules to help the network focus on the most relevant\ninformation when combining the features. Attention modules\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

This is especially true in enterprise settings, where automated agents hold the promise of a high impact. To fill this gap, we propose WorkArena++, a novel benchmark consisting of 682 tasks corresponding to realistic workflows routinely performed by knowledge workers.…\n▽ More\nThe ability of large language models (LLMs) to mimic human-like intelligence has led to a surge in LLM-based autonomous agents. Though recent LLMs seem capable of planning and reasoning given user instructions, their effectiveness in applying these capabilities for autonomous task solving remains underexplored. This is especially true in enterprise settings, where automated agents hold the promise of a high impact. To fill this gap, we propose WorkArena++, a novel benchmark consisting of 682 tasks corresponding to realistic workflows routinely performed by knowledge workers. WorkArena++ is designed to evaluate the planning, problem-solving, logical/arithmetic reasoning, retrieval, and contextual understanding abilities of web agents. Our empirical studies across state-of-the-art LLMs and vision-language models (VLMs), as well as human workers, reveal several challenges for such models to serve as useful assistants in the workplace. In addition to the benchmark, we provide a mechanism to effortlessly generate thousands of ground-truth observation/action traces, which can be used for fine-tuning existing models. Overall, we expect this work to serve as a useful resource to help the community progress toward capable autonomous agents. The benchmark can be found at https://github.com/ServiceNow/WorkArena.\n△ Less\nSubmitted 5 February, 2025; v1 submitted 7 July, 2024;\noriginally announced July 2024.","message":"The returned content is the main text content extracted from the page.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Previously, the\ndevelopment of transform-based tensor representation mainly\nfocuses on the transform aspect. Although several attempts\nconsider using shallow matrix factorization (e.g., singular value\ndecomposition and negative matrix factorization) to characterize\nthe frontal slices of transformed tensor (termed as latent tensor),\nthe faithful characterization aspect is underexplored. To address\nthis issue, we propose a unified Deep Tensor Representation\n(termed as DTR) framework by synergistically combining the\ndeep latent generative module and the deep transform module.\nEspecially, the deep latent generative module can faithfully\ngenerate the latent tensor as compared with shallow matrix\nfactorization. The new DTR framework not only allows us to better\nunderstand the classic shallow representations, but also leads us\nto explore new representation. To examine the representation\nability of the proposed DTR, we consider the representative multidimensional data recovery task and suggest an unsupervised\nDTR-based multi-dimensional data recovery model. Extensive\nexperiments demonstrate that DTR achieves superior performance\ncompared to state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative and\nqualitative aspects, especially for fine details recovery.\n\nObserved\n\nt-SVD\n\nHLRTF\n\nDTR\n\nOriginal\n\nPSNR 18.42 dB\n\nPSNR 30.01 dB\n\nPSNR 31.91 dB\n\nPSNR 33.95 dB\n\nPSNR Inf\n\nPSNR 15.77 dB\n\nPSNR 23.41 dB\n\nPSNR 30.41 dB\n\nPSNR 33.38 dB\n\nPSNR Inf\n\nFig. 1: The recovered pseudo-color images (R:10, G:20,\nB:30) by shallow characterization-based methods, i.e., t-SVD\n(based on SVD) [16] and HLRTF (based on negative matrix\nfactorzation) [17], and deep characterization-based method, i.e.,\nthe proposed DTR.\n\nHowever, the acquired multimedia data is usually incomplete\nowing to sensor failures or other abnormal conditions that\nIndex Terms—Deep tensor representation, deep latent generative module, deep transform module, multi-dimensional data occur during the acquisition and transmission process, which\ncan severely hinder subsequent applications [13]–[15]. Leveraging quantum mechanical principles, these machines\npromise superior efficiency and performance compared to classical counterparts, with potential applications in renewable energy and quantum computing. This paper investigates a quantum Otto\nengine operating in both ideal and finite-time scenarios, employing a two-level system interacting\nwith a harmonic oscillator within the framework of the anisotropic quantum Rabi-Stark model\n(AQRSM) as the working medium. This model is notable for exhibiting both first-order and continuous quantum phase transitions. By focusing on quantum heat engines, our study reveals that\nthese phase transitions critically modulate the efficiency and power of AQRSM-based engines, outperforming quantum engines fueled by working medium with harmonic spectrum. Additionally, we\nexplore the impacts of quantum friction and conduct limit cycle analysis in finite-time operations,\nproviding insights into optimizing quantum heat engines for practical implementation.\n\nI.\n\nINTRODUCTION\n\nQuantum thermodynamics stands as a rapidly advancing domain aimed at comprehending thermodynamic\nphenomena at the quantum scale [1–7]. This field attracts significant attention from the scientific community\nnot only due to its role in bridging quantum mechanics\nand thermodynamics but also for its potential in advancing devices that take advantage of the unique properties\nof quantum systems as their working medium (WM)[8–\n13].\nQuantum heat engines (QHEs) play a pivotal role in\nprobing the thermodynamic characteristics of quantum\nsystems and harnessing work from heat flow between hot\nand cold reservoirs [14–22]. They have attracted considerable attention from scientists and engineers due to\nthe growing demand for efficient energy utilization, storage, and transfer through quantum technologies. Experimentally, these engines have been implemented on various platforms, including trapped ion systems [23–26],\noptomechanics [27, 28], ultracold atoms [29, 30], and superconducting circuits [31–35].\nCritical systems, such as those undergoing quantum\nphase transitions, are particularly fascinating as WM in\nquantum heat engines due to their unique properties at\ncriticality, which have been extensively studied in recent\nliterature [36–45]. Near critical points, these systems exhibit heightened fluctuations and correlations, offering\nopportunities to enhance work extraction or gain better control over thermodynamic processes. This critical\nbehavior introduces intricate dynamics into the system,\nresulting in novel scaling laws for work output and efficiency. For instance, the divergence in correlation length\n∗ jsjin@dlut.edu.cn\n† gdmneto@zjnu.edu.cn\n\nand time near a quantum critical point profoundly impacts the engine’s efficiency. Moreover, investigating critical systems as WMs facilitates the exploration of universal properties of quantum heat engines. Universality suggests that different systems near criticality may\ndemonstrate similar scaling behaviors, thereby broadening the applicability of the results. This concept implies\nthat the performance of quantum heat engines can be\npredicted based on the critical exponents of the involved\nphase transitions [41].\nThe cornerstone model for light-matter interaction is\nthe quantum Rabi model (QRM), which involves a twolevel atom (qubit) coupled through dipole interaction to\na quantized single-mode cavity field [46–49]. This model\nhas been extensively explored across various domains,\nincluding quantum optics [50], entanglement generation\n[51], quantum thermodynamics [52–55], and the study of\nquantum phase transitions [56–60]. When different coupling constants are applied to the rotating-wave (RW)\nand counter-rotating-wave (CRW) interaction terms, the\nQRM transforms into the anisotropic quantum Rabi\nmodel (AQRM)[61–63]. Additionally, omitting the CRW\nterms results in the Jaynes-Cummings model (JCM) [64].\nNotably, both the AQRM and the JCM exhibit first-order\nquantum phase transitions (QPTs)[62, 65]. These QPTs\nsignificantly impact the quantum thermalization dynamics and the persistence of quantum correlations at thermal equilibrium [66–68].\nMoreover, the Quantum Rabi-Stark model (QRSM)\nis an extension of the QRM that incorporates nonlinear coupling terms[69–71]. This model was inspired by\nrecent experimental advancements[72] and has been extensively studied in theoretical works[72–75]. The QRSM\nhas been analyzed using the Bargmann space approach\n[71, 73] and solved using the Bogoliubov operator approach (BOA)[74]. These studies have revealed many\nexotic properties of the QRSM, such as first-order QPT\n\n\f2\nand spectral collapse [74]. Furthermore, the AQRM\nwith Stark coupling terms, also known as the anisotropic\nquantum Rabi-Stark model (AQRSM), has been found\nto exhibit both first-order and continuous QPTs [76].\nIn this paper, we perform a comprehensive examination of a thermal engine utilizing the AQRSM as the\nworking substance. Our study begins by thoroughly analyzing the ideal scenario of infinite operation time and\nzero power delivered, focusing on the intricate relationship between phase transitions and their influence on enhancing efficiency, work throughout the quantum Otto\ncycle. Additionally, we construct a detailed phase diagram to provide insights into the operational regimes,\nshedding light on the behavior of the coupled system\ncompared to engines operating with a qubit or a quantum\nharmonic oscillator.\nExpanding our investigation beyond the ideal scenario,\nwe explore finite-time operations, where the trade-off between power and efficiency becomes prominent, along\nwith the effects of quantum friction. Here, we highlight\nthe crucial role played by the spectral characteristics of\nthe working medium, including level crossings, degeneracies, and quasi-degenerate points, in shaping the performance of quantum engines. Furthermore, we incorporate\na limit cycle analysis to deepen our understanding of the\nsystem behavior under finite-time conditions.\nAcknowledgments to previous investigations on lightmatter heat engines[52–55], which considered the QRM\nas the WM, are due. However, these studies did not encompass first-order QPTs, which are absent in the QRM,\nand focused on the ideal scenario of zero power delivery\nand extremely low temperatures. Notably, only Ref.[55]\naddressed the second-order QPTs occurring at an infinite\nfrequency ratio for the Stirling cycle.\nThe paper is organized as follows: In Section II A, we\nintroduce the Anisotropic Quantum Rabi-Stark model,\ndescribe the Quantum Otto Cycle, and present the calculations for work and heat, as well as the operation in\nfinite time. In Section III, we present and discuss the results, focusing on the working operational regimes for the\nideal adiabatic process, efficiency, and work. Additionally, for the finite-time cycle, we focus on the Heat engine\nand perform an analysis of entropy production and how\nthe power and efficiency are affected by the light-matter\ncoupling. In Section IV, we present our conclusions.\n\nII.\nA.\n\nTHEORETICAL FRAMEWORK\n\nAnisotropic quantum Rabi-Stark model\n\nThe Anisotropic Quantum Rabi-Stark Model\n(AQRSM) describes the coupling between a twolevel system (qubit) and a single-mode bosonic field,\nexperiencing both the Rotating-Wave (RW) and\nCounter-Rotating-Wave (CRW) interactions asymmetrically. Additionally, it incorporates a nonlinear coupling\nterm between the atomic inversion and the cavity photon\n\nnumber. The Hamiltonian of the AQRSM[76] is given\nby (ℏ = 1)\n∆\nσ̂z + U â† âσ̂z\n2\n+ λ1 (âσ̂ + + â† σ̂ − ) + λ2 (â† σ̂ + + âσ̂ − ).\n\nĤ = ωâ† â +\n\n(1)\n\nIn the above, ω is the frequency of the bosonic field described by annihilation (creation) operator â (â† ), ∆ is\nthe transition frequency of qubit described by Pauli matrices σ̂x,y,z , σ̂ ± = (σ̂x ±iσ̂y )/2 . U denotes the nonlinear\nStark coupling strength, while λ1 and λ2 account for the\nqubit-boson coupling strengths for the RW and CRW interactions, respectively. One key aspect of the AQRSM\nin Eq.(1) is that it preserves the parity symmetry Z2 .\nThis fact can be evidenced by considering the total num†\n+ −\nber of excitations operator\n\u0010 N̂ \u0011= â â + σ̂ σ̂ and the\nparity operator π̂ = exp iπ N̂ . The conserved parity\nZ2 possesses two eigenvalues, ⟨π̂⟩ = ±1, depending on\nwhether the total number of excitations are even or odd.\nThe AQRSM is notable for its manifestation of both\nfirst-order and continuous quantum phase transitions\n(QPT)[76]. First-order QPTs are marked by abrupt\nchanges in ground-state properties during the transition,\nespecially in the infinite-volume (thermodynamic) limit.\nIn contrast, continuous QPTs exhibit smooth changes in\nground-state features, including the emergence of divergent length scales in quantum correlation functions and\na gapless energy spectrum in the infinite-volume limit.\nThe first-order QPT arises in the AQRSM at the energy crossing between the ground state and the first excited state, occurring when ω = 1 at\ns\nλ1c =\n\n∆(1 − U 2 )\n,\nU (1 + r2 ) + 1 − r2\n\n(2)\n\nwhere r = λ2 /λ1 represents the degree of anisotropy.\nThis transition signifies a substantial shift in the system’s behavior and is crucial for understanding its quantum properties. Continuous QPT, on the other hand,\noccurs in the QRM at an infinite frequency ratio of\n∆/ω[56]. At a critical coupling, the system undergoes\na second-order quantum phase transition into a superradiant phase, which has been observed in trapped-ion\nsettings[77], a phenomenon that naturally manifest itself\nin the AQRSM as well[58].\nInterestingly, AQRSM exhibits another form of continuous QPT, in a less restrictive physical realization,\nat finite frequency ratios ∆/ω for the special Stark\ncoupling |U | = 1. When U = ±1, AQRSM can be\nmapped into an effective quantum oscillator. The low\nenergy spectrum separates into two branches: the up2 2\nper one with E > ∓ ∆\n2 − 2κ α , and the lower one with\n2\nE < Ec± = ∓ ∆\n2 − 2α , where α = (λ1 + λ2 )/2 and\nκ = (1 − r)/(1 + r) ≤ 1. The real lower\nq spectra only\nexist before the critical coupling αc± =\n\n1∓∆±κ\n.\n2\n\n\f3\nAt the critical points αc± , the phenomenon known as\nspectral collapse occurs, where the discrete low energy\nlevels converge to a shared energy value Ec± , signaling\nthe closure of the energy gap within the system at the\ncritical coupling αc± . It is therefore suggested that gapless Goldstone mode excitations appear for αc± . Furthermore, beyond the critical point, the system experiences\nthe breaking of parity symmetry due to the infinite degeneracy of all states, characteristic of a second-order\nQPT. We note that the presence of QPT in the AQRSM\nmodel belongs to a distinct universality class compared\nto models that exhibit phase transitions in the infinite\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:98","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: DTR: A Unified Deep Tensor Representation Framework for Multimedia Data Recovery\nauthor: Zhou; Ting-Wei; Zhao; Xi-Le; Wang; Jian-Li; Luo; Yi-Si; Min; Bai; Xiao-Xuan; Yan; Hong\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.05267v1\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: Recently, the transform-based tensor representation has attracted increasing attention in multimedia data (e.g., images and videos) recovery problems, which consists of two indispensable components, i.e., transform and characterization. Previously, the development of transform-based tensor representation mainly focuses on the transform aspect. Although several attempts consider using shallow matrix factorization (e.g., singular value decomposition and negative matrix factorization) to characterize the frontal slices of transformed tensor (termed as latent tensor), the faithful characterization aspect is underexplored. To address this issue, we propose a unified Deep Tensor Representation (termed as DTR) framework by synergistically combining the deep latent generative module and the deep transform module. Especially, the deep latent generative module can faithfully generate the latent tensor as compared with shallow matrix factorization. The new DTR framework not only allows us to better understand the classic shallow representations, but also leads us to explore new representation. To examine the representation ability of the proposed DTR, we consider the representative multi-dimensional data recovery task and suggest an unsupervised DTR-based multi-dimensional data recovery model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DTR achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative and qualitative aspects, especially for fine details recovery.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2024-07-07\n---\nComputer Science > Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition\n[Submitted on 7 Jul 2024]\nTitle:DTR: A Unified Deep Tensor Representation Framework for Multimedia Data Recovery\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:Recently, the transform-based tensor representation has attracted increasing attention in multimedia data (e.g., images and videos) recovery problems, which consists of two indispensable components, i.e., transform and characterization. Previously, the development of transform-based tensor representation mainly focuses on the transform aspect. Although several attempts consider using shallow matrix factorization (e.g., singular value decomposition and negative matrix factorization) to characterize the frontal slices of transformed tensor (termed as latent tensor), the faithful characterization aspect is underexplored. To address this issue, we propose a unified Deep Tensor Representation (termed as DTR) framework by synergistically combining the deep latent generative module and the deep transform module. Especially, the deep latent generative module can faithfully generate the latent tensor as compared with shallow matrix factorization. The new DTR framework not only allows us to better understand the classic shallow representations, but also leads us to explore new representation. To examine the representation ability of the proposed DTR, we consider the representative multi-dimensional data recovery task and suggest an unsupervised DTR-based multi-dimensional data recovery model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DTR achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative and qualitative aspects, especially for fine details recovery.\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nPapers with Code (What is Papers with Code?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? Previously, the\ndevelopment of transform-based tensor representation mainly\nfocuses on the transform aspect. Although several attempts\nconsider using shallow matrix factorization (e.g., singular value\ndecomposition and negative matrix factorization) to characterize\nthe frontal slices of transformed tensor (termed as latent tensor),\nthe faithful characterization aspect is underexplored. However, existing studies\npredominantly focus on small-scale, simplified two-dimensional\nscenarios, often overlooking realistic environmental perturbations, motion dynamics, and inherent heterogeneity—factors that\npose substantial challenges to real-world applicability. To bridge\nthis gap, we investigate large-scale heterogeneous perimeterdefense game in a three-dimensional setting, incorporating realistic elements such as motion dynamics and wind fields. We derive\nthe Nash equilibrium strategies for both attackers and defenders,\ncharacterize the victory regions, and validate our theoretical\nfindings through extensive simulations. To tackle large-scale\nheterogeneous control challenges in defense strategies, we propose\nan Embedded Mean-Field Actor-Critic (EMFAC) framework.\nEMFAC leverages representation learning to enable high-level\naction aggregation in a mean-field manner, supporting scalable\ncoordination among defenders. Furthermore, we introduce a\nlightweight agent-level attention mechanism based on reward representation, which selectively filters observations and mean-field\ninformation to enhance decision-making efficiency and accelerate\nconvergence in large-scale tasks. Extensive simulations across\nvarying scales demonstrate the effectiveness and adaptability\nof EMFAC, which outperforms established baselines in both\nconvergence speed and overall performance. To further validate\npracticality, we test EMFAC in small-scale real-world experiments and conduct detailed analyses, offering deeper insights\ninto the framework’s effectiveness in complex scenarios.\nIndex Terms—Perimeter-defense Game, Large-scale Heterogeneous, Mean Field Reinforcement Learning, Agent-level Attention, Representation Learning.\n\nI. Our\napproach combines keypoint-based method with learnable pose\nregression through ‘keypoint-graph’, which is a graph representation of the keypoints. KGpose first estimates 3D keypoints for\neach object using an attentional multi-modal feature fusion of\nRGB and point cloud features. However, existing studies\npredominantly focus on small-scale, simplified two-dimensional\nscenarios, often overlooking realistic environmental perturbations, motion dynamics, and inherent heterogeneity—factors that\npose substantial challenges to real-world applicability. To bridge\nthis gap, we investigate large-scale heterogeneous perimeterdefense game in a three-dimensional setting, incorporating realistic elements such as motion dynamics and wind fields. We derive\nthe Nash equilibrium strategies for both attackers and defenders,\ncharacterize the victory regions, and validate our theoretical\nfindings through extensive simulations. To tackle large-scale\nheterogeneous control challenges in defense strategies, we propose\nan Embedded Mean-Field Actor-Critic (EMFAC) framework.\nEMFAC leverages representation learning to enable high-level\naction aggregation in a mean-field manner, supporting scalable\ncoordination among defenders. Furthermore, we introduce a\nlightweight agent-level attention mechanism based on reward representation, which selectively filters observations and mean-field\ninformation to enhance decision-making efficiency and accelerate\nconvergence in large-scale tasks. Extensive simulations across\nvarying scales demonstrate the effectiveness and adaptability\nof EMFAC, which outperforms established baselines in both\nconvergence speed and overall performance. Though recent LLMs seem capable of planning and reasoning given user instructions, their effectiveness in applying these capabilities for autonomous task solving remains underexplored. This is especially true in enterprise settings,…\n▽ More\nThe ability of large language models (LLMs) to mimic human-like intelligence has led to a surge in LLM-based autonomous agents. Though recent LLMs seem capable of planning and reasoning given user instructions, their effectiveness in applying these capabilities for autonomous task solving remains underexplored. This is especially true in enterprise settings, where automated agents hold the promise of a high impact. To fill this gap, we propose WorkArena++, a novel benchmark consisting of 682 tasks corresponding to realistic workflows routinely performed by knowledge workers. WorkArena++ is designed to evaluate the planning, problem-solving, logical/arithmetic reasoning, retrieval, and contextual understanding abilities of web agents. Our empirical studies across state-of-the-art LLMs and vision-language models (VLMs), as well as human workers, reveal several challenges for such models to serve as useful assistants in the workplace. In addition to the benchmark, we provide a mechanism to effortlessly generate thousands of ground-truth observation/action traces, which can be used for fine-tuning existing models. Overall, we expect this work to serve as a useful resource to help the community progress toward capable autonomous agents. The benchmark can be found at https://github.com/ServiceNow/WorkArena.\n△ Less\nSubmitted 5 February, 2025; v1 submitted 7 July, 2024;\noriginally announced July 2024.","message":"The returned content is the main text content extracted from the page.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Xu,\nYufan Song,\nBoxuan Li,\nYuxuan Tang,\nKritanjali Jain,\nMengxue Bao,\nZora Z. Wang,\nXuhui Zhou,\nZhitong Guo,\nMurong Cao,\nMingyang Yang,\nHao Yang Lu,\nAmaad Martin,\nZhe Su,\nLeander Maben,\nRaj Mehta,\nWayne Chi,\nLawrence Jang,\nYiqing Xie,\nShuyan Zhou,\nGraham Neubig\nAbstract:\n…life or work, and many aspects of work can be done entirely with access to a computer and the Internet. At the same time, thanks to improvements in large language models (LLMs), there has also been a rapid development in AI…\n▽ More\nWe interact with computers on an everyday basis, be it in everyday life or work, and many aspects of work can be done entirely with access to a computer and the Internet. At the same time, thanks to improvements in large language models (LLMs), there has also been a rapid development in AI agents that interact with and affect change in their surrounding environments. But how performant are AI agents at accelerating or even autonomously performing work-related tasks? The answer to this question has important implications both for industry looking to adopt AI into their workflows and for economic policy to understand the effects that adoption of AI may have on the labor market. To measure the progress of these LLM agents' performance on performing real-world professional tasks, in this paper we introduce TheAgentCompany, an extensible benchmark for evaluating AI agents that interact with the world in similar ways to those of a digital worker: by browsing the Web, writing code, running programs, and communicating with other coworkers. We build a self-contained environment with internal web sites and data that mimics a small software company environment, and create a variety of tasks that may be performed by workers in such a company. We test baseline agents powered by both closed API-based and open-weights language models (LMs), and find that the most competitive agent can complete 30% of tasks autonomously. This paints a nuanced picture on task automation with LM agents--in a setting simulating a real workplace, a good portion of simpler tasks could be solved autonomously, but more difficult long-horizon tasks are still beyond the reach of current systems. We release code, data, environment, and experiments on https://the-agent-company.com.\n△ Less\nSubmitted 10 September, 2025; v1 submitted 18 December, 2024;\noriginally announced December 2024.","message":"The returned content is the main text content extracted from the page.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Integrating AI…\n▽ More\nCustomer Relationship Management (CRM) systems are vital for modern enterprises, providing a foundation for managing customer interactions and data. Integrating AI agents into CRM systems can automate routine processes and enhance personalized service. However, deploying and evaluating these agents is challenging due to the lack of realistic benchmarks that reflect the complexity of real-world CRM tasks. To address this issue, we introduce CRMArena, a novel benchmark designed to evaluate AI agents on realistic tasks grounded in professional work environments. Following guidance from CRM experts and industry best practices, we designed CRMArena with nine customer service tasks distributed across three personas: service agent, analyst, and manager. The benchmark includes 16 commonly used industrial objects (e.g., account, order, knowledge article, case) with high interconnectivity, along with latent variables (e.g., complaint habits, policy violations) to simulate realistic data distributions. Experimental results reveal that state-of-the-art LLM agents succeed in less than 40% of the tasks with ReAct prompting, and less than 55% even with function-calling abilities. Our findings highlight the need for enhanced agent capabilities in function-calling and rule-following to be deployed in real-world work environments. CRMArena is an open challenge to the community: systems that can reliably complete tasks showcase direct business value in a popular work environment.\n△ Less\nSubmitted 16 February, 2025; v1 submitted 4 November, 2024;\noriginally announced November 2024.","message":"The returned content is the main text content extracted from the page.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Existing benchmarks often lack fidelity in their environments, data, and agent-user interactions, with limited coverage of diverse business scenarios and industries. To address these gaps, we introduce CRMArena-Pro, a novel benchmark for holistic, realistic assessment of LLM agents in diverse professional settings. CRMArena-Pro expands on CRMArena with nineteen expert-validated tasks across sales, service, and 'configure, price, and quote' processes, for both Business-to-Business and Business-to-Customer scenarios. It distinctively incorporates multi-turn interactions guided by diverse personas and robust confidentiality awareness assessments. Experiments reveal leading LLM agents achieve only around 58% single-turn success on CRMArena-Pro, with performance dropping significantly to approximately 35% in multi-turn settings. While Workflow Execution proves more tractable for top agents (over 83% single-turn success), other evaluated business skills present greater challenges. Furthermore, agents exhibit near-zero inherent confidentiality awareness; though targeted prompting can improve this, it often compromises task performance. These findings highlight a substantial gap between current LLM capabilities and enterprise demands, underscoring the need for advancements in multi-turn reasoning, confidentiality adherence, and versatile skill acquisition.\n△ Less\nSubmitted 24 May, 2025;\noriginally announced May 2025.","message":"The returned content is the main text content extracted from the page.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:40","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: KGpose: Keypoint-Graph Driven End-to-End Multi-Object 6D Pose Estimation via Point-Wise Pose Voting\nauthor: Jeong; Andrew\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.08909v1\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: This letter presents KGpose, a novel end-to-end framework for 6D pose estimation of multiple objects. Our approach combines keypoint-based method with learnable pose regression through `keypoint-graph', which is a graph representation of the keypoints. KGpose first estimates 3D keypoints for each object using an attentional multi-modal feature fusion of RGB and point cloud features. These keypoints are estimated from each point of point cloud and converted into a graph representation. The network directly regresses 6D pose parameters for each point through a sequence of keypoint-graph embedding and local graph embedding which are designed with graph convolutions, followed by rotation and translation heads. The final pose for each object is selected from the candidates of point-wise predictions. The method achieves competitive results on the benchmark dataset, demonstrating the effectiveness of our model. KGpose enables multi-object pose estimation without requiring an extra localization step, offering a unified and efficient solution for understanding geometric contexts in complex scenes for robotic applications.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2024-07-12\nlicense: CC BY 4.0\n---\nComputer Science > Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition\n[Submitted on 12 Jul 2024]\nTitle:KGpose: Keypoint-Graph Driven End-to-End Multi-Object 6D Pose Estimation via Point-Wise Pose Voting\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:This letter presents KGpose, a novel end-to-end framework for 6D pose estimation of multiple objects. Our approach combines keypoint-based method with learnable pose regression through `keypoint-graph', which is a graph representation of the keypoints. KGpose first estimates 3D keypoints for each object using an attentional multi-modal feature fusion of RGB and point cloud features. These keypoints are estimated from each point of point cloud and converted into a graph representation. The network directly regresses 6D pose parameters for each point through a sequence of keypoint-graph embedding and local graph embedding which are designed with graph convolutions, followed by rotation and translation heads. The final pose for each object is selected from the candidates of point-wise predictions. The method achieves competitive results on the benchmark dataset, demonstrating the effectiveness of our model. KGpose enables multi-object pose estimation without requiring an extra localization step, offering a unified and efficient solution for understanding geometric contexts in complex scenes for robotic applications.\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nPapers with Code (What is Papers with Code?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:41","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: Enhancing Cybersecurity in IoT Networks: A Deep Learning Approach to Anomaly Detection\nauthor: Pang; Yining; Li; Chenghan\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.08301v1\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: With the proliferation of the Internet and smart devices, IoT technology has seen significant advancements and has become an integral component of smart homes, urban security, smart logistics, and other sectors. IoT facilitates real-time monitoring of critical production indicators, enabling businesses to detect potential quality issues, anticipate equipment malfunctions, and refine processes, thereby minimizing losses and reducing costs. Furthermore, IoT enhances real-time asset tracking, optimizing asset utilization and management. However, the expansion of IoT has also led to a rise in cybercrimes, with devices increasingly serving as vectors for malicious attacks. As the number of IoT devices grows, there is an urgent need for robust network security measures to counter these escalating threats. This paper introduces a deep learning model incorporating LSTM and attention mechanisms, a pivotal strategy in combating cybercrime in IoT networks. Our experiments, conducted on datasets including IoT-23, BoT-IoT, IoT network intrusion, MQTT, and MQTTset, demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms existing baselines.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2024-12-11\nlicense: CC BY 4.0\n---\nComputer Science > Cryptography and Security\n[Submitted on 11 Dec 2024]\nTitle:Enhancing Cybersecurity in IoT Networks: A Deep Learning Approach to Anomaly Detection\nView PDFAbstract:With the proliferation of the Internet and smart devices, IoT technology has seen significant advancements and has become an integral component of smart homes, urban security, smart logistics, and other sectors. IoT facilitates real-time monitoring of critical production indicators, enabling businesses to detect potential quality issues, anticipate equipment malfunctions, and refine processes, thereby minimizing losses and reducing costs. Furthermore, IoT enhances real-time asset tracking, optimizing asset utilization and management. However, the expansion of IoT has also led to a rise in cybercrimes, with devices increasingly serving as vectors for malicious attacks. As the number of IoT devices grows, there is an urgent need for robust network security measures to counter these escalating threats. This paper introduces a deep learning model incorporating LSTM and attention mechanisms, a pivotal strategy in combating cybercrime in IoT networks. Our experiments, conducted on datasets including IoT-23, BoT-IoT, IoT network intrusion, MQTT, and MQTTset, demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms existing baselines.\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nPapers with Code (What is Papers with Code?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? However, systematic evaluation of these models across diverse robotic tasks remains limited. In this work, we present a comprehensive evaluation framework and benchmark suite for assessing VLA models. We profile three state-of-the-art VLM and VLAs - GPT-4o, OpenVLA, and JAT - across 20 diverse datasets from the Open-X-Embodiment collection, evaluating their performance on various manipulation tasks. Our analysis reveals several key insights: 1. current VLA models show significant variation in performance across different tasks and robot platforms, with GPT-4o demonstrating the most consistent performance through sophisticated prompt engineering, 2. all models struggle with complex manipulation tasks requiring multi-step planning, and 3. model performance is notably sensitive to action space characteristics and environmental factors. However, systematic evaluation of these models across diverse robotic tasks remains limited. In this work, we present a comprehensive evaluation framework and benchmark suite for assessing VLA models. We profile three state-of-the-art VLM and VLAs - GPT-4o, OpenVLA, and JAT - across 20 diverse datasets from the Open-X-Embodiment collection, evaluating their performance on various manipulation tasks. Our analysis reveals several key insights: 1. current VLA models show significant variation in performance across different tasks and robot platforms, with GPT-4o demonstrating the most consistent performance through sophisticated prompt engineering, 2. all models struggle with complex manipulation tasks requiring multi-step planning, and 3. model performance is notably sensitive to action space characteristics and environmental factors. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:43","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: Embedded Mean Field Reinforcement Learning for Perimeter-defense Game\nauthor: Wang; Li; Yu; Xin; Lv; Xuxin; Ai; Gangzheng; Wu; Wenjun\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14209v1\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: With the rapid advancement of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and missile technologies, perimeter-defense game between attackers and defenders for the protection of critical regions have become increasingly complex and strategically significant across a wide range of domains. However, existing studies predominantly focus on small-scale, simplified two-dimensional scenarios, often overlooking realistic environmental perturbations, motion dynamics, and inherent heterogeneity--factors that pose substantial challenges to real-world applicability. To bridge this gap, we investigate large-scale heterogeneous perimeter-defense game in a three-dimensional setting, incorporating realistic elements such as motion dynamics and wind fields. We derive the Nash equilibrium strategies for both attackers and defenders, characterize the victory regions, and validate our theoretical findings through extensive simulations. To tackle large-scale heterogeneous control challenges in defense strategies, we propose an Embedded Mean-Field Actor-Critic (EMFAC) framework. EMFAC leverages representation learning to enable high-level action aggregation in a mean-field manner, supporting scalable coordination among defenders. Furthermore, we introduce a lightweight agent-level attention mechanism based on reward representation, which selectively filters observations and mean-field information to enhance decision-making efficiency and accelerate convergence in large-scale tasks. Extensive simulations across varying scales demonstrate the effectiveness and adaptability of EMFAC, which outperforms established baselines in both convergence speed and overall performance. To further validate practicality, we test EMFAC in small-scale real-world experiments and conduct detailed analyses, offering deeper insights into the framework's effectiveness in complex scenarios.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2025-05-20\n---\nComputer Science > Artificial Intelligence\n[Submitted on 20 May 2025]\nTitle:Embedded Mean Field Reinforcement Learning for Perimeter-defense Game\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:With the rapid advancement of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and missile technologies, perimeter-defense game between attackers and defenders for the protection of critical regions have become increasingly complex and strategically significant across a wide range of domains. However, existing studies predominantly focus on small-scale, simplified two-dimensional scenarios, often overlooking realistic environmental perturbations, motion dynamics, and inherent heterogeneity--factors that pose substantial challenges to real-world applicability. To bridge this gap, we investigate large-scale heterogeneous perimeter-defense game in a three-dimensional setting, incorporating realistic elements such as motion dynamics and wind fields. We derive the Nash equilibrium strategies for both attackers and defenders, characterize the victory regions, and validate our theoretical findings through extensive simulations. To tackle large-scale heterogeneous control challenges in defense strategies, we propose an Embedded Mean-Field Actor-Critic (EMFAC) framework. EMFAC leverages representation learning to enable high-level action aggregation in a mean-field manner, supporting scalable coordination among defenders. Furthermore, we introduce a lightweight agent-level attention mechanism based on reward representation, which selectively filters observations and mean-field information to enhance decision-making efficiency and accelerate convergence in large-scale tasks. Extensive simulations across varying scales demonstrate the effectiveness and adaptability of EMFAC, which outperforms established baselines in both convergence speed and overall performance. To further validate practicality, we test EMFAC in small-scale real-world experiments and conduct detailed analyses, offering deeper insights into the framework's effectiveness in complex scenarios.\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? }\n    else if(window.matchMedia(\"(prefers-color-scheme: dark)\").matches) {\n      theme = \"dark\"; }\n    if (theme==\"dark\") {\n      document.documentElement.setAttribute(\"data-theme\", \"dark\");\n    } else {\n      document.documentElement.setAttribute(\"data-theme\", \"light\"); } }\n\n  detectColorScheme();\n\n  function toggleColorScheme(){\n    var current_theme = localStorage.getItem(\"ar5iv_theme\");\n    if (current_theme) {\n      if (current_theme == \"light\") {\n        localStorage.setItem(\"ar5iv_theme\", \"dark\"); Our approach combines keypoint-based method with learnable pose regression through ‘keypoint-graph’, which is a graph representation of the keypoints. KGpose first estimates 3D keypoints for each object using an attentional multi-modal feature fusion of RGB and point cloud features. These keypoints are estimated from each point of point cloud and converted into a graph representation. The network directly regresses 6D pose parameters for each point through a sequence of keypoint-graph embedding and local graph embedding which are designed with graph convolutions, followed by rotation and translation heads. The final pose for each object is selected from the candidates of point-wise predictions. The method achieves competitive results on the benchmark dataset, demonstrating the effectiveness of our model. KGpose enables multi-object pose estimation without requiring an extra localization step, offering a unified and efficient solution for understanding geometric contexts in complex scenes for robotic applications.</p>\n</div>\n<div class=\"ltx_keywords\">\n<h6 class=\"ltx_title ltx_title_keywords\">Index Terms: </h6>\nDeep learning, Object pose estimation, End-to-end network\n\n</div>\n<section id=\"S1\" class=\"ltx_section\">\n<h2 class=\"ltx_title ltx_title_section\">\n<span class=\"ltx_tag ltx_tag_section\">I </span><span id=\"S1.1.1\" class=\"ltx_text ltx_font_smallcaps\">Introduction</span>\n</h2>\n\n<div id=\"S1.p1\" class=\"ltx_para\">\n<p id=\"S1.p1.1\" class=\"ltx_p\">With advances in robot technologies, robots are gradually being utilized in industries and in our lives also. In order for robots to accomplish physical tasks effectively and safely along with humans, robots should understand the geometric relationships with their surroundings. One approach to understand it is to estimate 6D pose, which stands for 3D rotation and 3D translation, for each object in the scene and this information is crucial for robotic manipulation or grasping.</p>\n</div>\n<figure id=\"S1.F1\" class=\"ltx_figure\"><img src=\"/html/2407.08909/assets/figures/KGpose_digest.png\" id=\"S1.F1.g1\" class=\"ltx_graphics ltx_centering ltx_img_landscape\" width=\"538\" height=\"357\" alt=\"Refer to caption\">\n<figcaption class=\"ltx_caption ltx_centering\"><span class=\"ltx_tag ltx_tag_figure\">Figure 1: </span>Our approach. (a) Each point in input point cloud votes for 3D keypoints of each object in the scene. (b) Each set of the keypoints are converted into a graph, which is called ‘keypoint-graph’. (c) 6D pose of an object is estimated (or voted) from each point through several layers of graph convolution.</figcaption>\n</figure>\n<figure id=\"S1.F2\" class=\"ltx_figure\"><img src=\"/html/2407.08909/assets/figures/KGpose.png\" id=\"S1.F2.g1\" class=\"ltx_graphics ltx_centering ltx_img_landscape\" width=\"598\" height=\"200\" alt=\"Refer to caption\">\n<figcaption class=\"ltx_caption ltx_centering\"><span class=\"ltx_tag ltx_tag_figure\">Figure 2: </span>Overview of KGpose. Given RGB-D image, 3D keypoints are estimated through RGB and point cloud branches along with feature fusion process. Then, the estimated keypoints and corresponding keypoints from object model are converted into a graph representation and concatenated. These graphs are passed through keypoint graph embedding and several layers of local graph embedding stages to embed the graph features into each node (or point). Finally the embedded point features are fed into rotation and translation head to regress 6D pose parameters for each point, which are candidates of 6D pose. The final 6D pose for each object is determined by selecting the nearest candidate to the mean of the candidates of the object.</figcaption>\n</figure>\n<div id=\"S1.p2\" class=\"ltx_para\">\n<p id=\"S1.p2.1\" class=\"ltx_p\">Common approach for recovering 6D pose of an object is to regress 6D pose from keypoints of each object in the scene. Keypoint-based methods usually consist of two stages; first, estimate keypoints which could be considered as geometric clues for predicting object pose, then predict 6D pose parameters leveraging the estimated keypoints.\nSome methods detect 2D locations of the keypoints for each object and estimate 6D pose through RANSAC (RANdom SAmple Consensus) based Perspective-n-Point (PnP) algorithm establishing 2D-3D correspondences between the keypoints in the image and the ones from 3D object model<cite class=\"ltx_cite ltx_citemacro_cite\">[<a href=\"#bib.bib1\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">1</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib2\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">2</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib3\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">3</a>]</cite>.\nOther methods detect 3D keypoints rather than 2D ones. Estimating 3D keypoints could be preferred to avoid errors occurred from 2D projection, such as a reprojection error, a chance of overlapping of 2D projections from different keypoints in 3D space, or losing geometric constraints for rigid 3D objects<cite class=\"ltx_cite ltx_citemacro_cite\">[<a href=\"#bib.bib4\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">4</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib5\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">5</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib6\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">6</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib7\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">7</a>]</cite>. These methods usually detect 3D keypoints adopting a voting scheme<cite class=\"ltx_cite ltx_citemacro_cite\">[<a href=\"#bib.bib8\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">8</a>]</cite>, and determine 6D pose through least-squares fitting algorithm<cite class=\"ltx_cite ltx_citemacro_cite\">[<a href=\"#bib.bib9\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">9</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib10\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">10</a>]</cite> given the detected 3D keypoints in camera coordinate system and corresponding 3D keypoints in the object coordinate system.</p>\n</div>\n<div id=\"S1.p3\" class=\"ltx_para\">\n<p id=\"S1.p3.1\" class=\"ltx_p\">Recent pose estimation researches have focused on directly regressing 6D pose by designing fully differentiable models, which is referred to as an end-to-end pose estimation<cite class=\"ltx_cite ltx_citemacro_cite\">[<a href=\"#bib.bib11\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">11</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib12\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">12</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib13\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">13</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib14\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">14</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib15\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">15</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib16\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">16</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib17\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">17</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib18\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">18</a>]</cite>. There have been some methods that make RANSAC-PnP be differentiable<cite class=\"ltx_cite ltx_citemacro_cite\">[<a href=\"#bib.bib13\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">13</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib14\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">14</a>]</cite> to achieve end-to-end learning, while other methods try to regress the 6D pose directly from 2D-3D or 3D-3D correspondences<cite class=\"ltx_cite ltx_citemacro_cite\">[<a href=\"#bib.bib15\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">15</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib16\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">16</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib17\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">17</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib18\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">18</a>]</cite>.\nThe output of the model could be 3D rigid transformation matrix<cite class=\"ltx_cite ltx_citemacro_cite\">[<a href=\"#bib.bib17\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">17</a>, <a href=\"#bib.bib18\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">18</a>]</cite>, Euler angles with translation offsets<cite class=\"ltx_cite ltx_citemacro_cite\">[<a href=\"#bib.bib12\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">12</a>]</cite>, or quaternions<cite class=\"ltx_cite ltx_citemacro_cite\">[<a href=\"#bib.bib11\" title=\"\" class=\"ltx_ref\">11</a>]</cite>.</p>\n</div>\n<div id=\"S1.p4\" class=\"ltx_para\">\n<p id=\"S1.p4.1\" class=\"ltx_p\">We propose an end-to-end object 6D pose estimation framework that combines keypoint-based method with learnable pose regression module. While most of keypoint-based 6D pose estimation studies perform keypoint estimation, followed by pose regression based on the estimated keypoints, which is whether differentiable or not, we introduce a voting scheme for 6D pose estimation that generates point-wise pose prediction from input point cloud and learn to aggregate the candidates of 6D pose parameters to select the best one. We leveraged graph convolution on ‘keypoint-graphs’, which are constructed by converting estimated 3D keypoints into a graph representation, followed by disentangled 3D rotation and 3D translation head to regress the pose parameters.</p>\n</div>\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}
